question: Just reread the Demonic Ox. Thankyou for inserting updates on characters we met in previous stories. I hardly noticed them the first time because they didn't interrupt the flow at all. Pen's musings on a possible visit to the cantons intrigue me because I have a youngest sister who is "important" in her real life, but none of us seem to be able to remember that when she comes home. Have you considered writing that?
answer:
It is one of the dozen or three story ideas floating around like dead fish, not moving at the moment. With luck, one or a new one will revive eventually.

But, yeah, Pen's surviving family and old acquaintances back in Greenwell have no clue about his present accomplishments or stature, nor would they much relate even if they did.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Mitali
comments.text: I just read an excerpt from Penric's Demon in an unexpected context. Specifically, it was the scene where Des first speaks using Pen's mouth, which got me thinking of how far Pen has come - how far they have both come - since that fateful day in the Cantons. I'd love to read about Pen himself going back to those old places and marvelling at his life's journey.

question: In ref to a question below, I am still a bit puzzled by Pen's move from Martensbridge to Lodi. After I read Testimony (loved it btw), I thought that Pen applied because of Ruchia's relationship with Sassu, but now I remember that Ogial was the archdivine in Lodi. I though Pen had moved to Adria because he had met the Archdivine of that country at the Conclave, but this connection is attenuated it seems. Thoughts?
answer:
Pen moved to Lodi because he was fleeing the disastrous ending of his formal medical career and attendant emotional breakdown, and of course the loss of his position as court sorcerer, displaced by the new incumbent. Lodi was a place he'd heard about (including through Ruchia) and had some contact with its Temple hierarchy, from the conclave experience and other tasks for the princess-archdivine. So, not quite attenuated, Ogial being Sassu's direct successor, but not all that familiar to Pen either.

Also, it was a relatively close option in terms of travel, Pen being on a not-generous budget then. If his half-blind application letter there had not prospered, he'd probably have tried someplace in the Weald or Saone, next.

Also, it was supposed to be much warmer in Adria than the cantons.

Ogial, knowing Pen's reputation through reciprocal Temple contacts and possibly from Sassu, leapt on the chance to get Pen for Lodi, having a much higher opinion of Pen than Pen had of himself. The acceptance letter pretty much went out next-day-mail, as soon as the winter-blocked mountain passes permitted transit again.

Ta, L.

question: I met your books at 20, engineering student in Barcelona. Cordelia in *Shards of Honor* impacted how I dared take on impossible challenges—in work, in love. Rereading over decades, my understanding of her deepened with my own maturing. In my 40s could not finish GJ&RQ because I couldn't understand Cordelia in it. Now at 54, I love her there; From writer's side: how did your own life events impact Cordelia's evolution?
answer:
All my life experiences have informed all my characters, variously. It follows that my later-life experiences were unavailable to my characters when I was a younger writer -- Cordelia and I were near the same age when I began the book that ultimately became Shards of Honor. My vague ideas then of what an older Cordelia would be like and be doing proved quite different from what they eventually became, thirty years, many books, and a whole lot of series development later. (As was my own life.) I was thus relatively close to Cordelia's later age when I wrote GJ&RQ in the mid 20-teens.

As I recall, my original vision of a post-Aral Cordelia was that she would return to Beta Colony and finally have that daughter she'd dreamed of; only one though, because Beta Colony. She'd take up some more ordinary career, though she would discover Beta Colony really didn't fit her anymore. (Which it never had in the first place, or she wouldn't have been out with the survey service.)

The development of Sergyar as a setting gave both her and me a much more spacious and new frontier for her to inhabit, not just going backward to her mother's house. Older Cordelia, with all that further life experience under her belt, was much more self-assured and less inclined to put up with nonsense than I'd pictured earlier, finally able to claim a life for herself and not just one in service to others, a set of deeds she'd already fulfilled. She has a lot in common with post-Paladin of Souls Ista, that way.

GJ&RQ has been the most bifurcated of my novels in terms of reader response, although the reasons that readers come down pro or con vary, natch, with the readers. (Besides the perpetual and more universal, "This wasn't the book I wanted to have been reading!") You are interestingly positioned to see why, I suspect.

Ta, L.

question: I remember you mentioning getting into manga and anime a while ago. What are some of your current favorites?
answer:
Lately listed in full here:

https://www.goodreads.com/author_blog...

Ta, L.

question: I had all the copies of your books. After the Palisades Fire, I lost my house and all my collections. Now that Subterranean Press is closing. who will be publishing your books?
answer:
Sorry to hear about your fire!

SubPress or a designated successor will be publishing my Penric books through "Darksight Dare". Probably not any after that, though as I haven't yet written anything after that the question is a little moot at the moment.

All my stories, of course, are at present instantly available worldwide as ebooks on 5 different vendor platforms: Kindle, Nook, Kobo, Apple Books, and Google Play Books. Fireproof in their fashion, although subject to other vicissitudes.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: It’s good that all your works are available as e-books. This is not true of some other authors, who have strange gaps that I suspect are for weird legal reasons.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
E-books can, of course, be destroyed by fire, flood, or any other disaster that destroys whatever they’re stored on. In some cases, you may be able to get free replacements. In other cases, you can at least buy replacements. Or you can back up your files to some remote location unlikely to suffer from simultaneous disaster.

I’m fortunate that I prefer e-books. People who prefer paper books can’t easily create backup copies.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ JP --

Kindle, at least, retains backup copies which you can add/transfer to a new device. I've gone through a succession of several devices, and everything I've bought is still accessible as far as I know.

Yeah on weird legal reasons. Among them may be licenses for old books writers can't wrest back from publishers -- some of my own epub delays were due to waiting out these. Or the writer may be unable or unwilling to do the indie ebook route, or just not know how. Or be waiting to relicense the title to a new publisher along with some new work not yet written/finished.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Yes, I know Amazon will supply free replacements when you’ve bought the book once, but I don’t know about the other e-book vendors.

comments.author: Talli Ruksas
comments.text: I don't really buy paper versions of books to read anymore - just books for collecting purposes. But bookfinder.com is a great source for them.

question: Penric has learned how to do some shamanic magics, or at least approximate them enough through sorcery to require a blood payment. Would he be able to remove the spirit animal from the soul of a spirit warrior/shaman after death, or is that something only a real shaman can do, as spirit animals would seem to be under the purview of the Son? Thanks so much for the stories and answering all these questions.
answer:
This is untested. I suspect not, or sorcerers would have been deployed on that task heretofore.

Ta, L.

question: Great to see P&D adventures continuing! As a luddite print reader, any news on paper copies of Testimony of Mute Things or Darksight Dare? Will they eventually be offered by Subterranean Press (not yet listed); if so, do you have any schedule forecasts?
answer:
They are licensed to SubPress, and will be along in due course.

I don't have any firm schedule information at this time. "Testimony" has its cover complete and tip sheets all signed and turned in, so it should be along fairly soon. "Darksight Dare" is not yet in production, so that will likely be some time yet.

Ta, L.

question: The people of Pern & Darkover had wanted isolated, self-sufficient, low(er) tech pastoral/agricultural colonies, so when things went wrong they were better able to cope than if they were planning to maintain the same high-tech lifestyle-with galactic trade-they came from. Had you developed much about the intentions/planning of the Barrayaran colonists, and how that affected their situation when they got cut off?
answer:
Nope. What you have in the books is about what there is.

That said, there exists in my head a kind of mental corral, into which any details would have to fit. No, I'm not inclined to explore it.

Ta, L.

question: Probably a silly question but.. The earlier Audible books end with "this concludes the reading of ... a Penric and Desdemona novella in the world of the five gods". Newer ones don't add the WotFG reference. Do you know why it gets left off now?
answer:
Nope, no idea. I have nothing to do with Blackstone production decisions.

That said, I did switch my descriptors on the stories' subtitles, as the Pen & Des tales trained their audience, to just "A Penric & Desdemona Novella". Earlier, I thought folks needed more clues to help them figure out what they were getting.

And, of course, for "Penric's Demon", there wasn't a series yet for it to belong to, apart from that of the wider, previously established fantasy world.

Ta, L.

question: While reading Darksight I realized Dubro had died. I just did a complete relisten to the P&D books as I do when a new one comes out. In listening to Ox, Pen's daughters are discussing possible futures and speak about Dubro in the present tense. Then (a few weeks?) later Otta brings Tolga to talk to Pen and Tolga mentions missing Dubro. So did he die in that time window?
answer:
Must have.

Unless you wish to attribute it to writerly inattention, which most of my readers seem charitably unwilling to do. But yes, I did intend for him to die and pass along his demon to Rede in due course, along about this time.

It's actually about 4 months -- late summer to late fall. Or Penric wouldn't have been healed enough to manage even the modest activities of the tale.

Hanging out with the demon containing the image/ghost of someone you knew must be rather unsettling. Still more receiving it, poor Rede.


Ta, L.

comments.author: Mitali
comments.text: Oh! I noticed that Dubro had died (and that Rede now had Maska) in Darksight Dare, but didn't realize he had died so recently. Poor Dubro - he was such a sweetheart. I wish we could have seen him one last time.

question:
Hi there! I just read Shards of Honor for the first time and I loved it! Can't wait to read the rest of the series.
What I wanted to ask was, did ensign Dubauer, the one hit with the nerve disrupter, ever get any better? Slowly healing or advances in medical technology, maybe?
answer:
Welcome aboard! I hope you enjoy the rest of the ride. (Barrayar should be read next, in case you are wondering.)

My ideas about Dubauer now are probably a bit different from what I pictured in 1983 when I first wrote the book. Then I had a less optimistic outlook of him stuck as a 180-pound one-year-old forever, and needing care lifelong as such. Now I think he might have gradually been given/made more recovery, but never up to the high-end hotshot space explorer he was before. I picture him living in some sort of halfway house, and able to work simple but reasonably satisfying jobs in Betan horticulture/indoor gardening/food production. Much of his pre-injury memories, he never gets back, but is able to form new ones.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Thank you Lois for the updated future for Dubauer. I will certainly add this somewhat more optimistic outlook to my thinking about this story.

comments.author: Talli Ruksas
comments.text: Thank you! I love the idea that saving his life meant saving a future even if not the one he would have had. I always wondered if his mother considered cloning him since it's not taboo on Beta

question:
Ascendent Demons: A close friend had untreated psychosis; during episodes, their real personality was not present, much like a F/SF takeover by a different entity.

The excesses committed by Tigney were not prosecuted as crimes, but ascribed entirely to the demon, yes? Here, we prosecute the *body* of the offender, and some don't believe insanity is a defense. At least sorcerers can state a demon as fact....
answer:
I don't know that demon-Tigney committed any crimes as such, well, if you don't count running off with his body as theft or kidnapping. I fancy his demon's interests ran more to gluttony and sloth. (Any envy was all Tigney's.) Although there might have been ordinary theft, after the demon ran through all of Tigney's own money. It couldn't have included murder by magic, or his demon's spree would have been cut short. (Murder by ordinary physical means, but chosen by the demon, gets really iffy.)

But yes, a runaway ascendant demon's eventual Temple captors can sort out which crimes were whose. Convincing any victims of same would be another challenge, one of the many, many reasons this task is dreaded.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: The difference is that a demon can be definitely and permanently removed, but as far as I know insanity can’t be definitely and permanently removed. Responses to crime vary: sometimes a criminal is fined, as punishment, and sometimes imprisoned, to protect the public. If someone is prone to dangerous insanity, he/she should be imprisoned to protect the public. It’s not a matter of blame or punishment. Various animal species are also commonly imprisoned, not because they’re morally reprehensible, but simply because they’re a danger to ordinary humans.

question: I have been reading Damon Knight’s reviews and thoughts about SF in his book In Search of Wonder and I have two questions for you: (1) have you ever read it? and (2) have you met him? (Implying what were your impressions if so?) The book is now quite dated but the thoughts and analysis still apply - I especially like (and often detect, even in works that others think highly of) the “idiot plot” explanation.
answer:
I never met Damon Knight, and I've not (yet) read that book, perhaps embarrassingly as I did have the SFWA Grandmaster honor named for him bestowed upon me.*

I don't know if Knight coined the term "idiot plot" -- if so, it's long since escaped into the wild. In the version I encountered, it was described as a plot where all the characters had to be idiots to make things move forward. It is something I consciously try to avoid in my own writing, which does make ambushing the characters with plot-propelling events a bit harder.

Readers do tend to forgive it more when it results in some set-piece payoff of great emotional satisfaction, but it's better to get there without dumbing anyone down (including the villains.)

Ta, L.

* -- "Bestowed" is the right term for that one. A lot of writers are confused by literary awards being described as "won", as in a race or an election, when actually they are either the result of a poll, which is invalidated by efforts to sway it -- bad science to screw with your data! -- or given as a gift at the discretion of the givers. It took Younger Lois a while to figure this out, but hey, practice...

L.

comments.author: Shane Castle
comments.text: He says he thinks it was James Blish who coined the term, but then later he disses him a bit — I think because a book disappointed him. He also mentions the “second-order idiot plot,” wherein the entire society is composed of idiots, not just the principals.

question: I was re-reading Paladin of Souls the other day and wondered wistfully why there was never any follow up novellas as Ista takes on the task of removing stray elemental demons? And yesterday I was watching a cozy mystery and thought with a smile that locator Oswyl would make a great "detective" series.
answer:
I have had stray thoughts about Ista's future, but they are nothing so life changing as the events of Paladin of Souls. If life-fulfilling, perhaps. (And I did that idea once with Gentleman Jole and the Red Queen.)

Oswyl definitely has future adventures that we haven't seen. (And past ones as well -- he was 30, with 12 years of duty behind him, when we first met him in "Penric and the Shaman".) One of the 3 or 4 boxes still open in Pen's timeline labeled "Insert story here?" would be some task that comes up while younger Pen is a visiting student at the Royal College of Shamans, but so far no sufficient-to-me plot has reported for duty.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Barb in Maryland
comments.text: I amuse myself by imagining how some characters in Penric's shaman books are related to characters in The Hallowed Hunt.

comments.author: Mitali
comments.text:
I was wondering if Pen and Ista could possibly cross paths, but then had to admit that it's very unlikely, even with Pen's magically enhanced lifespan. :(
An "interquel" book featuring Pen visiting Oswyl et al. again would be great. Or, heck, Oswyl and Inglis could appear in the future, since there's already the potential plot hook of Rina going to the Weald to train as a shaman, with Pen (and Des) accompanying or visiting her.

question: Doing a full P&D reread before starting Darksight. I'm feeling we didn't get enough Llewen. She seemed a remarkable lady. No so?
answer:
Well, she was. "Testimony of Mute Things" was in part a chance to see a bit more of her. And Ruchia, of course.

If I ever do another tale set in Pen's Martensbridge years, she could show up again, but don't hold your breath.

Ta, L.

Ta, L.

question: How do the 5 gods apportion the souls they collect? The temple funeral ritual seems somewhat unfairly hierarchical; father, mother, son, daughter then bastard. Which begs the question, can a man be collected by the mother or daughter or a woman be collected by the father or son? The bastard seems gender neutral.
answer:
What order the gods are called on at a funeral is arbitrary.

"Apportion" seems a wrong term. To go to a god, a soul must be both chosen, and choose. The soul and the god also each have vetoes. Sort of like marriage, that way; both parties must consent for the union to be valid.

We see/hear about in "The Physicians of Vilnoc" the soul of the fort physician Master Orides going to the Mother, as do many other medical folk regardless of gender. Cazaril, obviously, eventually goes to his beloved and loving Lady the Daughter, despite most/all of the other gods doubtless holding out welcoming arms. All the gods are gender-neutral with regard to the souls They accept.

It isn't a matter of rules. (Though your questions sound as if you would be most comfortable with the Father, yourself.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Richard
comments.text: It might be more accurate to say that I have massive trust issues. :)

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Heh. OK.

Ta, L.

question: Are the 5 gods in the process of refining souls? If the worst of humanity is left sundered by those gods, that would imply that the collective pools of souls of the gods would be of slightly higher quality than the original pool. Thus, refined. Additionally, if the gods are using their pools of souls as a source of reincarnation, the aggregate quality of souls on the world would be getting slightly better over time.
answer:
Fascinating speculation, but wrong-headed. There is no reincarnation in the world of the five gods; the trip is one-time, one-way.

Souls are originally generated by living matter, and flow upward to the gods, becoming part of Them without losing their individuality, or at least not very fast. Although if you've had 100 years to develop in the world of matter, and 1,000 years to develop as part of the world mind/s, it's only natural the latter would come to loom larger in your being.

It might be slightly more correct to say the gods are being refined by the improvement of souls.

Ta, L.

question: If a story is told from a character's point-of-view but there are two people living in that character's head, is that story still considered a first person narrative?
answer:
Heh. I just explored that question in "Darkside Dare".

If the story were written in first person, "I went," "I said," etc. it would be trickier to handle, as both characters would be using the same pronoun, "I" and its cognates. In "Darksight Dare" I started out using one viewpoint per scene, all third person, but as Cinar and his passengers grew better acquainted, their viewpoints started to blend.

I've not yet attempted Des's very complicated viewpoint; Cinar-Iva-Itola-Spirit was a simplified version to try out.

So it would, perhaps, still be first person, but not single viewpoint.

Ta, L.

question: I've always wondered (and of course feel free not to answer if its not something you can share) if borrowing books from the library costs you a lot of income? I've always felt bad borrowing books from the library for authors that I enjoy and try to contribute by purchasing audiobooks or ebooks when i can afford it.
answer:
I think it's a wash. It gets my stories read, always a plus, and folks may remember them when they are more flush in the pocket. (Or even like them enough to buy their own copy, win!) Obscurity is much more of a hazard for modern writers than any other factor.

And the libraries do pay for them, after all.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I haunted libraries when I was young and had no money. These days I can afford to buy books, and I do. Furthermore, I now live in Catalonia, where library books tend to be in Spanish or Catalan.

comments.author: Shane Castle
comments.text: My mother was a librarian by training, and then married a man who became an officer in the US Army (1941). Wherever we got posted, she usually became one of the post's librarians. Army libraries were great - we had no fees to pay (except when returning books late) and there were tons of books available, without any censorship that I could see. My father retired in 1967, and I haven't been to an Army library since, so I have no idea what they are like now.

comments.author: Connie
comments.text: I relied on the local library for books when I was growing up and when raising my family. Money was tight and there was limited stores selling books near me.

comments.author: Christine
comments.text: A library introduced me to your Vorkosigan series via audio book and now I have all but 3 (on audio) and several paperbacks in my collection. I'm not as flush with cash as I used to be so when I buy a book I want it to be one I'll read again

comments.author: Talli Ruksas
comments.text: And I think libraries have to buy additional digital and audio copies after they've been checked out a certain number of times. I check out a lot of books but just buy all from LMB so I don't have to wait

question: Thank you for your writing; I’m loving the Penric series. My favorite might be Testimony of Mute Things for the Temple politics. The Archdivine of Orbas makes a brief appearance in that novella. In the later stories, is the same man still Archdivine of Orbas? And what is Penric’s relationship with him? Penric seems to have a lot of autonomy within the Temple hierarchy; is the Archdivine happy being hands-off?
answer:
The archdivine we see in "Masquerade in Lodi" is the successor of the one we meet in "Testimony". Whether Sassu has retired or has died in the interim is not specified; he might well still be around, if not for much longer.

We don't really see enough of Archdivine Ogial in "Masquerade" to know much of him, apart from him being a diligent Temple functionary, and his having jumped at Pen's job inquiry. Possibly advised by Sassu, who knows.

Powerful Temple sorcerers, like saints, are not so much hands-off as handled with kid gloves by their wiser seniors. Less-wise seniors might have occasion to learn better...

Though with Learned Tronio, in the later "Assassins of Thasalon", we might have an example of a sorcerer who was much too under-supervised, to disastrous results.

Ta, L.

question:
Thanks for your wonderful stories. I've read most of them, and enjoyed all I've read: up to #6 of Penric's adventures. He and his demon-ladies are a treat to hang around with! Most of all I like the humanity and decency of your main characters. They're like home, always.

Any words of wisdom for new authors trying to get their books to more readers? I'm indie-publishing now, learning to be a publisher as I go.
answer:
Glad you are enjoying my work!

I don't have any marketing advice; my career started too long ago, and in another world, for it to be a useful model for anyone else now.

There is no shortage of marketing advice on the internet; I get the impression some folks would rather write or read more of that than actual stories. The one thing that I have ever found to boost my work better than anything else I can do is to write another story, to the very best of my ability.

Pat Wrede just did an excellent cautionary post on the subject:

https://pcwrede.com/pcw-wp/horse-first/

Recommended reading. All her blog posts are levelheaded writing advice on all aspects of the game, and worth reading back through.

Good luck!

Ta, L.

comments.author: J.K. Stephens
comments.text: Thanks lots for your comments and care. About all the marketing advice that's abroad: agreed, it's a bit much. You can let it throw you off-balance as a writer, till all the joy goes out of the work itself. I'd rather spend most of my time doing the best writing I can, and meeting my readers that way... At your recommendation I'll be happy to read what Pat W has to say.

question: I just reread "Ethan of Athos" and wondered if you'd ever considered exploring the seemingly inevitable future genetic revolution on Athos?
answer:
No, I've left that one to the readers' imaginations.

As so much must be, in such a large and varied universe.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I like that book, I also reread it now and then, and every time it seems cut short because there’s no sequel. The ending is satisfactory enough for the people involved, it’s the story of Athos that seems unfinished. However, indeed, you can’t be expected to follow up the continuing stories of every inhabited planet; especially after such a length of time. “Ethan of Athos” was published 40 years ago.

comments.author: Manuel
comments.text: Thank you so much for answering so quickly! You are very generous with your fans and I appreciate it greatly.

question: Hi Lois, Longtime fan here. I started by picking up Diplomatic Immunity and then devouring everything you've written. Two quick questions ma'am 1. Is a full sized novel in the works re Rina's travels to the Weald with the whole family? 2. Do you see the demon queen that Ista faces in Paladin of Souls to be a dark mirror of Desdemona, to balance the good that Penric and Deedee have done? Awed regards, Keshav
answer:
I don't have a story about Rina's travels in process or in mind at this time. It's among the murk of possibilities circling the field, but I have no idea what might land nor when.

I wrote Paladin of Souls over a decade before the first Penric tale "Penric's Demon", so let's not get our causality mixed up. Princess Joen was definitely a dark mirror of Ista, but her demon was merely an example of a demon gone wrong by being in corrupted and corrupting humans and surviving far past its sell-by date.

If anything, Des was an exploration of more positive possibilities for a long-lived demon, when its succession of humans were all more-or-less goodhearted people. Whom I then had to make up, at least in sketchy outline.

Ta, L.

question: Forgive me if this has been answered before. You’ve said you consider the Vorkosigan Saga finished, and don’t feel you get back into it and do it justice. Have you ever thought about doing the prequel founding of Barrayar/Time of Isolation since it would be new characters & now you wouldn’t have to worry about contradictions or closing off story lines in the main timeline stories? Too depressing of subject matter?
answer:
This is not an idea that attracts me at this time.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
It’s something that could be done if you wanted to do it, but it would mean inventing a whole new set of characters, and putting them into a set situation with the future of their society already mapped out. From the author’s point of view, if you’re going to go to the trouble of inventing a whole new set of characters, it’s probably more interesting to put them into a more open-ended situation, where you have the freedom to go in any direction that takes your fancy.

As far as I can tell, currently you seem happy to go on writing more Penric & Desdemona stories, and I’m happy to go on reading them. That situation is not entirely open-ended, because we know that the Curse of Chalion will eventually come along later; but I don’t think it’s a big constraint on what happens in the meantime.

question: How does the Bastard compare to Earth Chaos/Trickster gods like Coyote/Loki/Anansi etc? As the god of chaos and both good & bad luck, even Pen hesitates to ask the Bastard for help. But there was a spot in “Demonic Ox” that seemed to imply that the Bastard isn’t being capricious or causing mischief just for fun, but can’t Himself control the type of luck He brings when He intervenes, it’s a roll of the dice?
answer:
The Bastard has trickster aspects, but is a much vaster and, shall we say, more numinous being than the jumped-up superhumans of some Earth mythologies. Present, like all the five gods, in all places and all times, singularly and simultaneously, the bodiless Minds of the World. Any individual human, no matter how spiritually sensitive, can only take in a tiny fraction of the whole of a god with their own limited mind, though with a generally alarming sense of how much more lies beyond, unperceivable.

Human post hoc reasoning being what it is, it's another good question how much of the luck attributed is caused by the god's Thumb (very little) and how much is simply taking advantage of events that occur (parsimony.) The gods mostly don't control, but even though they are inside time (and, thus, not omniscient in the usual somewhat tortuous theological sense) with their omnisentience they can certainly predict near-future events much better than any person could, and can sometimes share that knowledge.

Ta, L.

question: Hi Lois. I am a Canadian fan and I LOVE your Penric & Desdemona series. I see you have recently released "Darksight Dare", book 16 in the series. However Amazon.com will not allow me to purchase the ebook and Amazon.ca does not have it. Will this title be released to the Canadian market? Thanks!
answer:
You are jumping the gun, though not by much. As of today, 04/19/26, the novella has not yet been released. I expect it to go up sometime in the next two weeks, on all 5 of our vendor platforms -- Kindle, Nook, Apple Books, Kobo, and Google Play Books. They will all go up approximately simultaneously, within a few days of each other. One or more will be certainly be available in Canada.

I'm looking forward to it, too!

Ta, L.

question: Are the books Penric creates the printing plates for sold for profit by the Temple, or distributed free to help spread knowledge as a Temple mission? Free to temples, sold to private individuals? Would anything Penric creates plates for be considered Temple property, or could he create something to sell for himself, like a book of his adventures, or to teach the general public about demons, “Demons for Dummies”?
answer:
The answers to all these questions is "Yes", variously. "Demons for Dummies" is an interesting idea; Pen would certainly be all for it, if it ever occurs to him.

Ta, L.

question: How much leeway do sorcerers have for non-Temple employment? It seems the standard for kings/dukes, etc to have a court sorcerer, but what about private individuals? Could merchants employ a sorcerer to protect caravans/warehouses/manufacturing facilities? Is there such a thing as a wandering sorcerer for hire, or would they be considered a hedge-sorcerer as they wouldn’t be under direct&constant Temple supervision?
answer:
As ever with the world of the five gods, the answers can vary with time and place.

A wandering sorcerer for hire would be a hedge sorcerer, unless s/he were a former Temple sorcerer gone walkabout. Such persons (of both sorts) exist.

This question gets addressed in the upcoming "Darksight Dare", actually. Sorta.

Ta, L.

question: At the time of “Demonic Ox” what is the financial situation of the Penric household, compared in-universe, and to a modern American standard? They seem to have enough, but not any extravagance, ie sufficient food but very basic. Rina seems concerned about it, but is the frugality her parents building up enough money for apprenticeship/education/marriage portion? How much does the weaving contribute to the household?
answer:
It's hard to compare with modern times, as the underlying economy and technology is so different. Very much not 1:1, especially with respect to clothing, which was proportionately much more expensive then. So Nikys's fabric production and sewing, even just for her own household without the occasional piece sold on, is a very much bigger financial help than it would be now.

I suppose Pen's Temple/ducal stipend would be about equivalent to an assistant professor at a junior college. Frugality, but not poverty.

The post does come with some valuable perks -- free mail service, and free use of the Temple horses or coaches when he needs to travel out of town on the Order's or the duke's business, and a certain amount of free or at least unstructured time to work on his own projects, which in turn bring honor to his employers.

Note that the kin Juralds lease, but do not own, their city row-house.

Pen may not be doing much squirreling away of funds for the kids' future needs, but I guarantee Nikys is. Household finances, both short and long term, are all pretty much her direct wheelhouse.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Thank you for the details about the finances in these books. I agree, different standards must apply. And, given his feelings about his brother not finding funds to allow him his chance at university education, I feel sure that Pen (and Des too) are thinking about ways to allow his children good options for their future, even if they make choices that aren't exactly as he had envisioned. As the Princess Arch Divine suggested at the end of Fox, there are ways to profit from some of his publications, perhaps?

comments.author: Denise Dykstra
comments.text: Did Adelis ever go back to their childhood home and retrieve Lady Florina's jewels for Nikys? I thought she wanted to have a home that they owned.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Denise --

I feel the jewels must have been retrieved at some point, by Aldelis or Ikos or both, and are being held safely wherever, probably by Tanar, for Nikys's future needs when called for. I may *cough* have plans for them, but I'm not ready to address them yet.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Lois, what a hopeful *cough*, I have been hoping that someday you might develop a plot that included Lady Florina's jewels. But then, any Pen and Des (or other) tale that you decide you want to write I feel sure I will enjoy reading.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
I should add, with respect to Nikys's fiber arts, a rec for Elizabeth Wayland Barber's excellent book, Women's Work: the First 20,000 Years.

Ta, L.

question:
Hi, I've been writing a script version of Curse of Chalion and I would like your opinion. Is there anyway I can send it to you (or the publisher)? And is it something you would be interested in?
It works out to be about 9 episodes in a TV series (HBO or NetFlix?) but there is some interpretation of the original story that might need approval.

Peter
answer:
No, alas, you cannot send it to me, nor my publisher -- HarperCollins does not have the media rights to this work.

You would need to take your script to a bona fide media production company with some kind of track record for successfully completed projects, and get them to license the work through my media rights agent.

It took me quite a while, as a newbie writer, to get it figured out that works are not licensed to scriptwriters, who are generally as broke as any other writers, but to producers, the people with the hundreds of millions of dollars in hand and years worth of experience and contacts to actually make anything. They, not writers, are who decides what gets made and shown. It's something of a closed shop, and generally requires someone with insider industry experience to penetrate.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Peter Mitchell
comments.text: Thanks for your response. Regardless of rights (I will see if I can chase that up some other way) would you be interested in seeing it? I understand that it would take time away from real work (more stories!)

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
For legal reasons, I can't. Sorry.

L.

comments.author: Peter Mitchell
comments.text: Understood. On the remote chance anything comes of it (it is really just a hobby project) I would like your input. If you can't now, then so be it.

question:
Hi Ms Bujold!

I’d love to read your World of the Five Gods series, but it doesn’t look like there’s a uniform edition of the 3 books currently in print. The Baen omnibuses are beautiful… any plans for them to reissue The Curse of Chalion, Paladin of Souls, and The Hallowed Hunt in similar editions?
answer:
HarperCollins still hold the licenses for the Chalion novel trio, so any reprinting would be up to them. Which, in general, a publisher doesn't do for elderly backlists, unless there is a big related frontlist book to pair them with, which is most unlikely to happen.

Happily, HarperCollins still keeps them widely available as ebooks, and also in paperback -- being so old, they won't spontaneously show up in bookstores, but they can be special-ordered. Otherwise, the used market still has many-many copies of the original quite beautiful HarperCollins hardcovers slopping around. (Well, Curse is a bit hard to find.)

Among other places, try Uncle Hugo's Science Fiction Bookstore here in Minneapolis, which does keep them in stock. Signed, even. And does mail order.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Carolyn
comments.text: We love Uncle Hugos! That's where we go to find our Lois books!

comments.author: Janice
comments.text: I recently found the set as Kobo ebooks in the subscription service.

question: Would Betan gene-cleaning include removing neurodivergence/mental health conditions like autism/ADHD/depression/bipolar etc with a genetic basis? Miles did mention toning down the ADHD in his children. Do Betans feel that some neurodivergence can provide helpful alternatives, space pilots maybe? Or does Miles’s mental conditions come strictly from his Barrayan side? He worries about Mad Yuri’s genetic legacy.
answer:
Probably, but one must also posit that such things should be MUCH better understood a thousand years from now. Early 21st C. mental/psychological models may not apply, any more than, say, cosmology models from circa 1000 CE apply today.

I would also point out that gene-alterations with that degree of futuristic expertise can go both ways. If you take something out, and then decide you want it back, you could likely get it restored.

While Miles's mental conditions certainly stem in part from his parents, that's not just genetic, being nurture as much as nature. Not to mention all that trauma from the soltoxin, its antidote, and any other monkeying around his Barrayaran docs were trying to keep him alive before he was even born. Miles's mental conditions come from his everything.

As do ours...

Ta, L.

question: I saw a FB post that was asking for SF/F books with autistic characters, and someone suggested Cordelia (with a callout of Miles for ADHD/bipolar/perhaps mild DID). While Cordeila self-describes as socially awkward and is forthright to the point of bluntness, and may have missed some social maneuvering (the ship command posting with her ex; high Barrayaran society) I never felt that went into autism. Your thoughts?
answer:
I feel that such diagnostic terminology is best reserved for conditions that are actually impairing the individual's ability to cope with life. Which is certainly not Cordelia.

That said, she is very much a science geek girl in her personality type.

Ta, L.

question:
Hi Lois,
We had a brief conversation one here about T Kingfisher a few years back and you mentioned you were keen to read the sequel to Swordheart
It is now coming out in July
https://uk.bookshop.org/p/books/daggerbound-t-kingfisher/8c400e5bb016809d?ean=9780356530888&next=t
Just in case you hadn't heard.
(?)
answer:
Yep! I have it on preorder.

Ta, L.

question: Dear Lois I am 79 years old , and still reading your vorkosigan saga with unchanged pleasure. I am still working finding reasons to do it I was not even immaginating. Just to say that it is not impossible to rise your pen on a new book of the Saga. let me/us surprise shall you?
answer:
Alas for you and the many who have asked this question before, I don't have anything in mind Vorkosiganly at the moment. For now, I'm still finding more to do with Penric and his Problems, which remain happily (for me) nicely varied. (Also, the novella length suits my semi-retirement semi-energy levels.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: The Vorkosigan family was my first love in your books, and I still revisit. But Pen and his extended family may have overtaken them in my heart. Pen is such a very NICE person. While still being complex enough to be interesting. And Des is wonderfully complex. And all of the rest of his friends and family so interesting.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: The prospect of more Penric always pleases.

question: I've just discovered your Vorkosigan books and Miles has already stolen my heart completely. He's reminding me of my first and most enduring book crush, Lord Peter Wimsey - brilliant, perennially underestimated, naturally gifted at mysteries and espionage with a gorgeous romantic streak. I'm wondering if you ever read Sayers? Give Miles my regards, I feel like he'd be pleased to make a fictional heartthrob shortlist.
answer:
I discovered Sayers and Wimsey nigh on half a century ago, to my lasting delight. I can't remember if the books or that initial BBC TV production from back then came first for me, but I sopped up all the volumes I could find thereafter. I haven't revisited in quite a while, though.

When you get as far into the Vorkosigan series as A Civil Campaign, I think the dedication will amuse you. (Though do read in chronological order, if you can. https://www.goodreads.com/author_blog... for your aid.)

By the way, I understand that the Plot Trysts crew, who did a long series of podcasts on the Vorkosigan books last year, are planning one on Sayers sometime in the future. Folks might keep an eye out for it.

https://plottrysts.wordpress.com/meg-...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Thank you for mentioning the Plot Trysts plans for Sayers. I very much enjoyed their discussions about the Vorkosigan books.

question: (2/2) and I was honestly beyond shocked to stumble across Pen going through the same thing. It truly felt serendipitous and special, and I can't wait to finish them all. I suspected that you had a medical background based on the realism of the pathology in the book - and I wondered if you incorporated this aspect of Pen's character because of going through similar things? Thank you for writing
answer:
[Questioner wrote earlier] "...I am a resident physician and have struggled a lot throughout my career with burnout and work related distress. [Penric's tales] quickly became ... a tremendous joy and anchor in difficult times with work."

Lois: Back in my increasingly distant 20s (the 1970s) I worked as a drug administration tech in a large university hospital, cycling though a number of different nursing units in patient care. Mine was not a high-level responsible position like a nurse or doctor, but I accumulated a lot of human observation without exactly realizing it.

As a bookish, slightly spectrum-ish person who mainly lived up inside her own head, I was not a very good match for the job (which of course I did not realize at the time, nor for long after) and after several more years than I had expected to hold it, I couldn't wait to get out of there. So that, plus my general background in biology and reading/watching pop sci as a long-time science fan, has certainly informed both the Penric tales and my other works.

Amusingly, part of the inspiration for "Physicians" was a kid's biography book I read back in 5th grade about Walter Reed figuring out Yellow Fever. Nothing is ever wasted when one is a writer. Though it may take half a century to pay off...

I've also tapped physician friends and fans for crit on my drafts, as needed.

I may add, the Subterranean Press cover for "The Physicians of Vilnoc" (which I think of as "Pen's thousand-yard stare") is my all-time favorite piece of cover art in any series of mine.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jérôme Coulomb
comments.text:
((subterraneanpress[dotcom]/the-physicians-of-vilnoc/))
and https:// must be added before
Sorry for the cryptic link... goodreads don't let you enter links, for security.
Very nice cover art indeed.

question: Hello, I just wanted to pass along thanks for the wonderful Penric & Des stories. I am a resident physician and have struggled a lot throughout my career with burnout and work related distress. I started reading these books recently because I love fantasy and kind, helpful main characters, and they quickly became my impossibly special to me and a tremendous joy and anchor in difficult times with work. (1/2)?
answer:
I'm glad you found my tales a support in a tough job!

Split questions and answers tend to get separated on Goodreads, so I'll try to recap your essential information in my next bit.

Ta, L.

question: I've been a huge fan since I read The Warrior's Apprentice at 10-11. At primary school they let me choose the book to read one semester and I did Borders of Infinity. Miles' books are at fault for my english, since there were no translations available yet in Spanish as new books came out and I just went ahead and tried reading them. Will you keep doing Penric&Desdemona novellas (love them) or will you make novels too?
answer:
Glad you have enjoyed my books for so long, and across languages too!

I will probably do more Penric -- he's a very flexible character who can feature in many kinds of stories, plus providing enjoyable headspace/s for me to be in as a writer. I only plan and work on one tale at a time, so length and subject, characters and even genre are potentially up for grabs every outing. So, no commitments here.

My lower energy levels these days continue to find the novella length more manageable, at least to entice myself to start a project. What happens to the plan once it's in motion is always negotiable, depending on what the story's needs turn out to be. The Assassins of Thasalon for an example.

Side note, many of my titles are now widely available in Spanish translation as ebooks -- check them out on Kindle, the Apple Bookstore, Nook, Kobo, and Google Play Books. A search for my name will usually turn them up, generally farther down the list. I also have some in French and a couple of other languages.

Ta, L.

question: Lois, I just want to say thank you! I've reread almost all your books over the years, and have many favorites. Your Penric series, though, even more than the others, helped me get through the pandemic. The opening of Penric's Demon is my go-to when I need a calming treat. I love your scenery as much as your characters. I'm giving my grandson "The Warrior's Apprentice" for his 15th birthday! ?
answer:
Thank you so much for your years of support! I hope your grandson has fun too.

Ta, L.

question: What do the braids look like in The World of The Five Gods series? Are they a 6 or 8 or 9 inch strip hanging down from the shoulder? Or are they worn across the shoulder like epaulettes or are the looped under the arm like a military braid? How are the different shades braided? Are they twisted if only two or knotted? Is the pin a simple straight (sewing) pin or is it a brooch?
answer:
Temple ornament varies from realm to realm. And, given that everything is handmade locally, will probably even vary from from town to town in less overt ways. Braiding styles will also vary, but are usually 3 or 4 strands, different colors encoding different information depending on god, sub-specialty, and rank. No 2-strand twists; if two colors, there would be 2 strands of one color and 1 of the contrasting one, etc.

The Adriac braids Pen wears are like military braids, a long braid re-looped under the arm and/or off one shoulder. (Number of loops is also an opportunity for variation.) Orbas does not have braids, but rather colored sashes.

The pin can't be straight -- too insecure. Closed or safety-like pin or a broach would be sensible, plus the pin gives another chance to encode information. Fancier or jeweled for higher ranks (or just richer divines) or a gradation of metals seems possible. If there is any way to distinguish and advertise status and hierarchy, people will use it.

Ta, L.

question: ANY CHANCE YOU WILL CREATE MORE mILES HIS COUSIN OR MOTHER THESE HELPED MY RECOVERY FROM cANCER and i lost many trying to find Paperback replacements because that is how i started reading SF and always will any more Vorkosigan PLEASE?
answer:
As before, I have nothing going in that direction at this time. But I'm so glad my books helped you get through a hard time! That's one of my fondest hopes for my work.

A general note, all my titles are widely and instantly available as low-priced ebooks on 5 vendor platforms. Their Blackstone audio editions are also available widely. Baen also still has some titles new in stock in paper editions. Used copies vary in availability, but Uncle Hugo's Science Fiction Bookstore in Minneapolis is always good to try, and they do mail order. Folks can try there for paperback replacements.

Ta, L.

question: Miles fears the haut will one day consider the rest of the galaxy less than human, implying not worthy of existence. Do the haut worry THEIR descendants might consider them not really haut and wipe them out, or would they consider that proof they’ve succeeded in creating a superior race?
answer:
As not even the Cetagandans have conquered death, by the time such descendants came along, their progenitors would all be dead, rendering your concern for their concern rather moot.

Ta, L.

question: In Barrayaran stories does Baba Yaga ride in a mortar used for grinding, or given it’s Barrayar, a munitions launcher? Mortar launchers got the name because early ones resembled bowls, but I found a picture showing Baba Yaga riding in a cylindrical mortar resembling modern launchers.
answer:
It being Barrayar, the most probable answer is "all of the above."

Ta, L.

comments.author: Spriggana
comments.text: In the wiki under in the entry for mortar go to the "Husking and dehulling" part - there is a photo of a quite large mortar most likely used for husking rice. Quite a big one, not fly-worthy for a Baba Yaga, but maybe her kid ;-).

question: How much of a shock would it be to be chosen by a god you didn’t expect when you died, especially the Bastard? Could that be a cause of sundering if the person rejected the god as not being the one they wanted, or would they just be happy to be chosen by a god?
answer:
Well, for life after death, the gods are the only game in town. If you don't want any of the 5 choices, the 6th is dissolution (not the fun kind) and nonexistence.

Going off in a snit because you didn't get your first choice is certainly an option, if a short-term one, free will and all that. You have until enough of you evaporates to be beyond assent to change your mind, then it's over. Amount of time varying by the individual and initial conditions. For the Weald warriors trapped in the Wounded Woods, it ran to ~400 years, but that case was an extreme outlier. For most people, it's a matter of days.)

Ta, L.

question: It seems the Daughter’s spheres would include rebirth, transformation, state change: ice thaws; baren fields turn green; a man broken in body, mind & spirit is reborn. Perhaps a saint of transformation IS rather than DOES? Maybe a miracle of transformation is more subtle and internal?
answer:
Subtle and internal is certainly the mode of most of the 5GU magic, yes.

Tough on the cover artists.

Ta, L.

question:
In World of the Five Gods, devotees wear the color of their chosen god. However, people of the appropriate age/status also tend to wear those colors. 

In real life, people wearing a red shirt are mistaken for Target employees, etc. Do random widows ever get grabbed/importuned to act as a healer or midwife? People wearing a white shift get ignored/warding signs?
answer:
Possibly. There is a great deal of variety across countries and regions about the signs and ornaments of religion, which I might need to make up if any of my characters get to new places. The Father/Mother/Son/Daughter are fixed as to imagined gender, but the Bastard can be conceived as male/female/both/neither, depending. Leaving aside the innate dubiousness of assigning any sex to beings that never had bodies.

Ta, L.

question: Hi Lois! One thing thats come up a few times in the stories is peoples' surprise at learning the Bastards order has saints. That implies that they're familiar with saints of other orders. What are saints of the Mother, Sister, and Brother known for? You've touched on the saints of the Father - though whats the difference between a saint and a petty saint?
answer:
Saints of the Mother are known mainly for healing and female fertility, which puts them in very high demand and makes them more visible to the public. The Father's arena includes justice, therefore law, truth, and correctness, which may sometimes also make them more visible. (Though I don't imagine someone getting divine inspiration in abstruse mathematics would make much of a splash outside their own specialty.) Also male fertility, likewise more a matter for private prayer or visits to the Mother's physicians.

I've not much worked on saints of the Brother, though the shaman Scuolla was as close to one as anyone could be. I've likewise not worked out much for the Daughter, though we did meet one great one, Cazaril, trained up for one great deed. Lines between areas of saintly doings are by no means rigid or impermeable, as we saw with Umegat and the healing menagerie that was staving off the effects of Orico's diabetes.

Petty saints have small apertures in their souls to let gods through, now and then. Greater saints have larger ones, though "now and then" may still apply. Consider the difference between a sink faucet and a fire hose. So, difference of degree not kind.

Ta, L.

question:
Hi Lois!

In a previous Q&A about music you mentioned Dave Carter and Tracy Grammer, and Enya's "Cu Chulainn".

I really enjoyed both - something about D & T's Native American/Buddhist vibes fits well with the landscape of the Sharing Knife. 

So my q - do you have any more music recs? In general, and pieces that might make good accompaniments to your work?

Cheers and thanks so much for your books!
Julian
answer:
I've used music quite a bit for inspiration in my earlier years; not so much nowadays, in part for hearing loss but mostly for not having so much time doing mindless physical chores -- I don't want music when I'm reading, writing, nor, obviously, watching TV. Driving, when I also used to pick up new music from the radio, is also much reduced, and my kids and their then-new music moved out long ago. I am not yet adapt at sorting streaming music, though I've finally mastered earbuds.

So, not much new to report past the era of Enya and Dave Carter, nor, reaching even father back, Steeleye Span or Mannheim Steamroller. I have picked up some assorted new-to-me songs from watching fan music vids, but they've not translated much beyond me rewatching their vids, and I haven't been tracking back much to the song origins to fix the original artists in what passes for my memory. Dessa, maybe? Taylor Swift? Fanvids are quite a random grab-bag.

Ta, L.

Added: was reminded of this: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ga_jw...
Bringing the circle all the way around, like the Worm Ourobouros.

question: Hello, my parents worked for your father at OSU. They have spoken highly of him my entire life. My mother was his secretary in the 60’s. I’m reaching out to express their respect and admiration. My mother is 90 and my father 87. They do regret never returning to visit your father. I hope he knew he was always remembered fondly? Rich Hudec
answer:
Thank you for passing this on!

I do have dim memories of secretaries coming out to our house in that period to assist in the assembling of The Handbook of Non-destructive Testing. Self-centered child as I was, I can't pull up names or faces at this late date, but I do remember all the stacks of paper involved, in the downstairs room on Henderson Road devoted to the project at one point. And. of course, in his upstairs office, the clack of my dad's IBM Selectric mixed with pipe smoke and classical music from the OSU radio station that he played while working. I remember lying on the floor in front of that radio and watching the hot orange glow of the vacuum tubes.

(And also, once, at a somewhat younger age, applying my cat's paws to the ink pad for stamping things, and turning it loose to walk across the typescripts on the desk. I don't think this crime was ever brought home to me...)

I've no idea if your parents ever encountered me, wandering around in the background in the 50s and 60s, but give them my best wishes.

Ta, L.

(More on my dad here, for those wondering: https://www.dendarii.com/tribute.html )

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text:
I have wondered if, like Miles, you might wish that your father be remembered primarily for his connection to YOU, rather than the other way around.
One can wish this while yet realizing the father was a great man, I think.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ CV --

Well, that thought of Miles's didn't come out of the air... :-)

I could wish my dad had lived to see more of my success, as he died in 1986 just after my first book was published, and before any evidence could accrue. I did have one favorable review from The Chicago Sun-Times to show off before then, for which I will always be grateful.

Ta, L.

question: Wishing everyone a happy Winterfair and Father's Midwinter Day (if I have the name correct) and whatever midwinter celebration there may be in the Wide Green World. I assume that most cultures have some way of marking the longest night and longest day or some time close to this? I have very much enjoyed all three of these worlds.
answer:
Thank you so much for your kind wishes!

(Actually, winter solstice is just the Father's Day. Father's Midwinter would be halfway between winter solstice and spring equinox, i.e., around Groundhog Day, Feb. 2. Not that the months have to have the same number of days as in our world, nor the days the exact time duration... Pretty close though. But any planet with axial tilt should have seasons, solstices, and equinoxes, which make them a pretty good universal fantasy calendar marker.)

Ta, L.

question: Over 1,500 answered questions (a few of them mine!) and no search function so far as I can tell ... but I re-read The Hallowed Hunt, and wanted to know if there was more to the erstwhile sorcerer and now petty saint of the Bastard Lewko's story (if I read the novel correctly)? I hope that they both knowingly sacrificed his demon in some grandiloquent act of heroism and sacrifice, instead of usual chaos run amok...TY!
answer:
Yeah, lack of a search function here is a trial. I've sometimes had luck using just plain old Google, plus enough assorted unique key words. Ferex, if you google "Was Shards of Honor ever a Star Trek fanfic?" it will take one to my posted Goodreads Q&A answer explaining no, not.

Anyway, back to the Five Gods. It was not, alas, grandiloquent heroism that lost Lewko his demon, it was overwork, trying to do too hard a job/jobs of uphill magic too fast until his demon violently rebelled. So, chaos run amok, but also misjudgment, mismanagement (by both Lewko and his seniors) and perhaps a touch of hubris. (Demons are not, generally, into self-sacrifice.)

The hard-learned humility that followed for Lewko probably had something to do with his later ability to channel his god, but I've never worked out the details.

Ta, L.

question: Recent articles have discussed "Extra-Uterine Fetal Incubation" or "Artificial Wombs" and I slid down the rabbit hole of the various possible impacts of those terms and others as compared to "Uterine Replicators". For one, I was struck by how the former might imply superiority over the original in ways the latter does not. What considerations, if any, do you recall making when you chose your term?
answer:
As near as I can remember from a thought-stream 42 years ago, I made the term up on the fly for the scene in Shards of Honor in which the tech first appears, and Cordelia is explaining it to Aral (and the reader.) I wanted a term which was not that used in prior SF works -- Huxley's Brave New World most obviously, but there were others, including Zelazny's Lord of Light. (I don't offhand now remember what terms those authors used in their books without looking them up.) I wanted my term for it to have an inherently obvious meaning to the readers, so I didn't have to spend a lot of time teaching new pseudo-vocabulary or neologisms.

I didn't at the time push any particular opinion over which method was "superior" -- a loaded term -- although if one is a human of the female reproductive persuasion, the advantages and disadvantages become more obvious the more one thinks about them, which I did later on. Most immediately in Ethan of Athos, though also touched on in the ensuing Falling Free. And then explored further from time to time throughout the series, all the way up through Gentleman Jole and the Red Queen.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Dex
comments.text: we miss Miles too

comments.author: Dex
comments.text: what do you think of Earthers Saga by S.H. Jucha?

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Dex --

Don't know the work, so, no opinion.

Ta, L.

question:
Hello Lois,
I'm just starting my umpteenth re-read of the Vorkosigan series, and I wanted to ask you whether the end of Shards of Honour was written later than the rest? Perhaps when you wrote Barrayar, as I thought I understood that there was a gap. Apologies if you've already discussed this at length somewhere. The end of Shards is quite strikingly beautiful to me in its depth of human psychology.
answer:
Assuming you are asking about the codicil chapter "Aftermaths", it was written soon after Shards, somewhere around or just before the time of starting The Warrior's Apprentice. (Recall that the first ~ 8 chapters of what later became Barrayar were written back in 1983 before I broke off and started writing Warrior's in 1984.)

I was still trying to break in at this time, so sent the short story around to the then-extant SF magazine markets, along with a few others I was circulating in hope, where it did not sell, but got completely contradictory rejection letters. (A lesson to me it took a while to figure out.) By the time in late 1985 that the 3 finished novels all sold to Baen, it was still kicking around unsold.

I forget what phone conversation I was in with Jim Baen when the discussion came up of what he called his "paperback magazine", Far Frontiers, monthly short story collections packaged as and marketed with Baen's mass market paperback offerings. Anyway, his idea came up of pubbing the story there a month or two before Shards was launched, to introduce this new writer Bujold to his audience. (Jim schemed a lot about how to launch new writers.) I also don't remember if it was his idea or mine to append the story to the novel as an afterword, but anyway, that was when it joined the text, after the manuscript was sold to Baen but before the novel was published.

Anyway, good call; I think it strengthened the thematic weight of the novel ever after, as you experienced.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Rachel
comments.text: Actually, I haven't even gotten back to Aftermaths yet. It's chapter 15 I was wondering about. It seems like Shards could have wrapped up with Cordelia and Aral reuniting, or marrying, or Elena's birth--but the scene where Ezar calls Aral back in is what I was wondering about. I'd wondered whether it was written later as sort of a lead-up to Barrayar, but it sounds like the answer is no. The whole of chapter 15, from Ezar talking to Aral, through Aral giving Koudelka his tabs, to Aral telling Cordelia, "Naturally not. Fountains keep nothing for themselves," is really emotionally impactful for me. Bravo, and thank you. Hard to imagine that it was early work.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Rachel --

Ah, OK. That's different.

Chapter 15 was me just writing along, feeling out my story as I went. It was where I found the best stopping place when I circled back looking for one, when I stalled out after the soltoxin attack, and really wanted to get a finished piece out to maybe sell.

In the first draft, Drou was introduced in that Ch. 15 section, but I cut her in the final edit because the now-last-chapter was no place to be introducing entirely new characters.

Also in the first draft the story had gone on to what is now Chapter 2 of Barrayar -- when I pulled those carbons out of my attic and tackled the story again, I wrote a new first chapter to ease readers in, and reintroduced Drou.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Christopher Winter
comments.text: Interesting! Was Drou still introduced by Kareen, but in some event separate from the ball? Or introduced by some other character? Perhaps Negri...Negri lived large in the series without a speaking role.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Chris --

Negri probably, consequent to the regent appointment.

Drafts are long gone, so I can't look it up.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Christopher Winter
comments.text: Ah, too bad the drafts are gone...I was hoping for bootleg outtakes, which are all the rage in music now (a "Cordelia's Honor" boxset!). Were they actually thrown away or simply lost in the vagaries of life?

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ CW --

Sold, actually, to a collector/dealer back in the mid-90s... can't remember his name now. ...Goes and looks it up... Barry R. Levin. I expect he's long gone now. No idea where they went after that.

All paper material prior to winter of 2013-14 when I last moved went to the U. of Northern Illinois, who had a collections librarian, Lynn Thomas, who came and got them in a van, bless her. More recently I've been shifting my wastepaper, er, manuscripts, to the U. of Minnesota, also via a librarian willing to come get them.

In general, I would not want my old outtakes brought to light -- there were good reasons some were deleted.

Ta, L.

question:
Hi, not a question but I'm following you on X and your recent activity there has been a little... questionable! I think your account may have been hacked...
Question mark because the form requires it?
answer:
I am not, and never will be, on X. I am only on Goodreads. That person is a scammer/impersonator.

I am told I can only get them thrown off X if I sign up myself, which I am not willing to do. I was able to get one (no telling how many scumbags are doing this) thrown off Mastodon with the help of a reader there.

Please tell everyone on X and elsewhere you can reach that this is an impersonator, and I blog only on Goodreads.

A good recent SFWA Writer Beware post tackled the problem more generally --
https://writerbeware.blog/2025/11/14/...

My own ones have been reported to me before --

https://www.goodreads.com/author_blog...

https://www.goodreads.com/author_blog...

Please repost these links wherever the matter next arises! Especially the SFWA one, which is very informative.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Addendum -- I see the SFWA post has added info about how to unmask impersonators on X using the geolocation function. Do check it out if you are on X/Twitter.

And do please pass that SFWA link along!

Ta, L.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Also, if someone on X does do the geolocation check, do report what you find here in the comments. I'm curious.

Ta, L.

question: Rather belatedly, I find myself wondering whether “Penric’s Fox” was at all inspired by the use of foxes in the Rivers of London series. Of course, there may be no connection at all (and the fox has made no second appearance). Would you care to comment?
answer:
The foxes in "Penric's Fox" were directly observed and inspired by the family of foxes who took up residence under my garden shed for two wonderful springs, to the great benefit of all my garden plants suddenly not being chewed down to the roots by rabbits. Six or eight fuzzy fox cubs tumbling over one another playing out beside the shed were a dose of cuteness (I will not say overdose) that had even the neighbors stopping to watch. I wish the foxes would come back, and the rabbits be their lunches... Alas, only those two years.

Something else is under there this year but I haven't seen what. Suspect it's raccoons, but, despite also being fun to watch if you catch them in daylight, can carry distemper or rabies, and leave problematic droppings.

So, no connection to the Rivers of London, fun reading though that series is.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Thanks! I occasionally saw foxes around my late mother’s home on the western edge of London. I read that they’re common in Catalonia, where I live, but I don’t think I’ve seen any here. I’ve occasionally seen wild boar, which sometimes venture into towns. Several of them were recently filmed interrupting a game of football.

question: Where in the timeline of P&D stories does Testimony fall? It must be earlier since the story includes the Princess-Archdivine but other than that I don't know.
answer:
Well, I did explicitly give Pen's age -- 27, was it? Might have been 26. A couple of years before Llewen's death, and Pen's mother's. He was 30 when he first reached Cedonia, so must have been ~29 during his year in Lodi/Adria.

For a few more markers, he was 19 when he contracted Des, ~22 when he graduated seminary. 23 - 24, for "Shaman" and "Fox".

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: At one point, Penric mentions that he contracted his demon 8 years ago, and he was 19 then, which would make him 27. However, at another point he mentions that Ruchia’s last trip to Lodi was 19 years ago, and he would have been about 7 then, which would make him about 26 now.

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text: I've taken to writing Pen's age in pencil on the first page of each book. Helps me keep track.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
There is up to a year's slack in any character's relative age depending on whether one pictures them born in January or December, or the local equivalent. I've not yet needed to establish Pen' birthday, so approximations serve.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I think the inaccuracy arises primarily because Pen is measuring time in approximate years without counting months or days. When he says, “X years ago”, I suppose he may be rounding to the nearest year, or rounding up, or rounding down. Or he may be subtracting one year from the other without paying any attention to the months—which I guess is most likely as it’s the simplest method.

question: I enjoy the Penric & Desdemona series very much! I wondered if there is an audiobook planned for "Testimony of Mute Things"?
answer:
I can now say that there is! Blackstone contract is supposedly en route, and will get here when it gets here.

Word is they will try to move production along briskly, and have Grover Gardner as narrator again. Couple of months, maybe? Average the lead times of the last three between epub and audio release, and you can likely get a good estimate.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Alex
comments.text: Oh, good. I love Gardner's narration.

comments.author: Janet
comments.text: Great! These days, I'm buying both the Kindle and the Audible copies. I love the synchronization between them.

comments.author: Jérôme Coulomb
comments.text: yes !!! Great reader! And perfect for Penric and Desdemona!

question: I'm a bit confused about Umegat's groom, Daris. At the end of the book, it seems implied that Daris was the man Umegat fell in love with, when he was in the Roknari islands. But Umegat says that man was hanged, so what is Daris's story?
answer:
Umegat's first love was indeed horribly executed. Daris was a later love. Who also ran into grave perils, presumably on some joint spying mission, possibly against the Golden General's forces, but at least he survived. There might have been a heroic or at least clever rescue by Umegat in there somewhere.

Ta, L.

question: I’d like to wish you a pleasant birthday, as you join the rest of us ‘49ers. I recently baked a cake for my spouse - her birthday was Oct 26, and she wanted a peanut butter cake with chocolate buttercream frosting. She got it. I hope your birthday wishes are granted. (Obligatory: ?)
answer:
Thank you!

My birthday is going pretty well, starting with living to have it... Otherwise a nice quiet Minnesota fall day, remote relatives reporting in by phone, my son to arrive with fancy pizza for dinner in due course. My best present came a little early, with "Testimony of Mute Things" topping out in the 80s in the top 100 of the Kindle store yesterday. (Which commonly happens the 2nd weekend of sales, but still nice.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Happy birthday, enjoy your pizza, and thank you for the latest adventure of Penric and Desdemona!

comments.author: RavenT
comments.text: Happy birthday!

comments.author: Brzk
comments.text:
Bestest of Birthdays, Lois! (Should be... the wrong time of the day on your side already but these things can span weeks if celebrations go really out of hand!)
I've made a carrot cake in honour of my grandma's name day some 6-7 weeks ago, it's sort of customary on the Balkans to celebrate and commemorate everything with food (as is the case on the rest of the planet).
Do you have a favourite cake??

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Bzrk --

Carrot cake and very dark chocolate are my favorite cakes, though alas my metabolism can't handle buttercream or other sweet frostings anymore. No-sugar-added whipped cream still works...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Brzk
comments.text:
Oh :) !
Dark chocolate is my favourite too, my metabolism shifts them to "all the right places" as they say... directly.
I find that cream cheese frosting works very nice and is somewhat lighter than buttercream but will give it a try with a very dark chocolate with no-sugar-added whipped cream very soon.
Thank you!

question: On a re-read I noticed right at the end of Penric & the Fox when Pen is telling Llewen about Baroness kin Pikepool's idea for expanding Pen's plates into a printing press etc & Llewen tells Pen to have her secretary "explain the concept of a percentage recompense to you." but I don't remember this being addressed again. Does Pen have a big pile-up of royalties & income waiting in an account for him back in the Weald?
answer:
Ah, glad someone noticed that.

I fancy that modest sums are forwarded to Pen now and then. This will get harder to effect the farther he moves away. Medieval international banking is a fascinating real-life study. But usually money transfers came down to finding some hopefully trustworthy person going that way, giving them the purse, and hoping they didn't run into disasters along the route. Erratic method.

One of the interesting anecdotes from my great-something uncle the ironclad riverboat medic in the American Civil War was about entrusting his year's pay to be taken home to New Hampshire by hand, and it never arriving. I'm not sure what $500 translates to in today's dollars, but that loss had to have hurt.

(See: The Gerould Family of New Hampshire in the Civil War: Two Diaries and a Memoir on Kindle.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I tried to Google the question, and it seems that $500 in the early 1860s would be worth approximately $10,000 now. Although I think the calculation of inflation relies on pricing a basket of goods and services. The contents of the basket are determined subjectively, and what’s in there will change gradually over time, so it’s all very inexact. Common items available today weren’t available at any price in the 1860s.

question: Next year, 2026, marks 40 years since your first books were published in 1986. Congratulations on four decades of actively writing and publishing so many enjoyable and impactful tales! Are you or anyone else making special plans to celebrate this upcoming milestone?
answer:
Not yet!

(Padding for effect, I often count my career as starting in late 1982, when I first started writing those books that were first published. But nothing says I can't have two (career) birthdays. Like Miles.)

Ta, L.

question: The Sharing Knife maps are remarkably similar to the Midwest of North America. This has made me wonder occasionally whether it's really the far future of our own world - if one assumes a technological culture was succeeded by one based on groundwork, which then fell. Or is this an alternate America, like Patricia Wrede's Frontier Magic?
answer:
It's an entirely alternate world, not our far future. I just borrowed some prefab worldbuilding.

Hey, if Terry Pratchett can do it with the Chalk, I can do it with the Ohio River...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Rick Ellrod
comments.text: No harm in prefab worldbuilding! We've all got to get our inspiration from somewhere.

question: Hello! Minor Vorkosigan question here, but all of the armsmen whose family names we learn are proles not Vor. Is the status of armsmen barred to Vor (due to conflicting oaths), or were armsmen historically of the Vor caste and the prevalence of proles a consequence of the opening of the military and new institutions like ImpSec and the municipal guard?
answer:
