comments.text: Fortunately, I find your books infinitely re-readable. I don't understand people who claim never to revisit a book. Do they only listen to a song once?

comments.author: Donna McMaster
comments.text: I've read the series a few times over the years, and have never been bored. Last year I discovered that my local library had the audiobooks for the Miles series, so I listened my way through them over the course of a week or two. I recommend that as different way to experience the books. They're well done and I find that an audiobook makes chopping vegetables, washing dishes, and other routine tasks go a lot faster. :)

question: Spoiler for the beloved Vorkosiverse (Thank you so much!) .... .... ... .. Plans for Cordelia and her girls? The neighbors?
answer:
Any plans for Cordelia's girls are pretty speculative, beyond them being six very different persons, growing up in a very science & sea oriented dual household with their half brothers, as Cordelia & Oliver become a Sergyar survey science team of note, with joint careers spanning 6 or so decades. (Think, I dunno, some cross between Mary & Louis B. Leakey and Jacques Cousteau?) It would be amusing if this second half of her life becomes the part most remembered in wider Nexus annals.

I did have a thought the other day re: Selig & Simone, who have always been the blank or wild cards among Miles's kids -- I suspect they may end up on Sergyar, actually. (I wonder if Cordelia will ever succeed in getting the planet renamed? Couple generations on it's probably as moot as the Americas being named for a dead Italian geographer.)

Ta, L.

question: Regarding spirits in the World of the Five Gods: Are spirits more common in areas where the majority of the population follow the Quadrene faith, since they are more likely to reject the Bastard? Or do the other 4 gods "pick up the slack" so to speak?
answer:
If by spirits you mean sundered souls, perhaps a little to both. The gods in general prefer to take up all the feasible souls they can, sort of the opposite of the old Calvinist "theory of the Elect", where only a tiny fraction of the absurdly virtuous (by Calvinist standards) go to heaven, all others rejected.

As we have seen, the Quadrene divines find ways to stack the deck against reporting souls gone to the fifth god, er, demon-god, but that's hardly the same thing.

As we've also seen, souls of the dead come with a best-by date, after which their deterioration renders them unable to meaningfully assent. But it does build in a short buyer's-remorse period where one might get over one's first embarrassed rejection of a god or gods. Or not, as may be.

Ta, L.

comments.author: PaxAee
comments.text: Thanks a lot!

question: I was reading through the questions you've answered on Goodreads, and I was struck by how much your answers read as if written by Desdemona herself. I mean that in the best possible way, as I find her quite funny. I know all the characters are technically "you", but I was wondering if there may be a little more "you" written into Desdemona than other characters?
answer:
Mm, perhaps. I wrote in the introduction to the collection Penric's Progress: "In the world of the five gods, magical powers are not inborn but obtained, by—among other methods—acquiring a chaos demon, an invisible bodiless entity stemming ultimately from the fifth god, known as the Bastard in His holy family. This led me to the need to make up the character of the demon, which, in this system, opened up a huge range of possibilities, since demons, all beginning as identical blobs of escaped chaos, learn their diverse personalities from the succession of sorcerers they symbiotically inhabit over, potentially, many lives. This also, to my vast amusement, gave me the chance to combine the fetching young male protagonist beloved by the market with the cranky older woman so familiar from the inside of my own head."

None of my characters are me, but they do borrow from my experiences, as from a lending library. Or as a cup of sugar from the neighbor, or junk from the attic, or a literary garage sale, whatever.

(Beware, by the way, the common misperception that only female characters are imprinted this way from a female writer. Or only male ones from a male, I expect. Creativity routinely escapes such restrictive social roles.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Brok3n
comments.text:
Your final remark reminds me that in her Acknowledgnents for /Six of Crows/, Leigh Bardugo wrote:

I have a degenerative condition called osteonecrosis. This basically translates to “bone death,” which sounds kind of gothy and romantic, but actually means that every step I take is painful and that I sometimes need to walk with a cane. It’s no coincidence I chose to create a protagonist struggling with similar symptoms, and I often felt that Kaz and I were limping along this road together.
Kaz, for those who haven't read SoC, is male.

comments.author: Brzk
comments.text: ... that the flow of experiences between author and readers, and characters, is ... shared ?! Although authors seem to receive the inferior product of unrefined feedback from their audience. Can we say - the denser the experience, the better the author? And very rarely - as is the case with Lois's work - the magic is there.

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text: Since Desdemona resided in Lois's head for years before she passed Des on to Penric, we could think of Lois as one of Des's previous riders, much like Ruchia.

comments.author: Brzk
comments.text: @Conniption - Quite!! :X

question: I noticed that: Miles ends up married only when he is being rescued (from a murder trial), Duv ends up roped into the groats circle, when he is in danger of his life (Delia's big brick), Mark and Aral are both (arguably) rescued from impending self-destructive mental instability. Baz Jesek is vaguely comparable, as is Enrique Burgos. Ivan (and By) don't fit the same mold. Was that intentional?
answer:
No?

They are characters in books. There's supposed to be more drama than in real life.

:-), L.

comments.author: Spriggana
comments.text: Ivan’s wife, on the other hand… ;-)

question:
Do we need a nag Lois Bujold campaign? LOL

100,000 readers send letters to her house.

Just kidding! But I am still waitng for the Lord Autitor to deal with aliens.
Dr. Riva invents technology to link to more distant wormholes.
answer:
Alas, no sentient aliens in the Vorkosiverse. (Though we have seen other biota -- apparently, one can't get an oxygen atmosphere without it.) This is a genetic engineering future, like Cordwainer Smith's Instrumentality, where 10,000 years down the timeline all the exotic aliens will be descended from us. We are seeing the first stirrings of this future in Miles's time. (I strongly rec the cycle of stories of Cordwainer Smith, some of which have aged surprisingly well from when I first met them in the late 1960s.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Martha
comments.text: Yeah, the aliens in the Vorkosigan universe are the Cetagandans. :)

comments.author: Mary Berg
comments.text: Cordwainer Smith is an amazing author. I have read all of his stories and turned quite a few people on to him as well. He's hard to find though. Not everything has made it online.

comments.author: Susan Price
comments.text: Mary and Lois, I agree with the recommendation for Cordwainer Smith -- thought provoking (and hard to find)

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
A quick glance in the Kindle store under the author's name finds a short collection, The Best of Cordwainer Smith, as the first hit, so not that hard to find or obtain. But he wrote a lot more than that. NESFA Press had a complete-works version on paper, many years ago -- I don't know if that's still available.

Ta, L.

question: Sadly, the most likely thing. But it would be interesting to see people's reactions if Penric was the one who DIDN'T stand out (physically at least). Maybe someone else from the cantons will visit him and verify that his tales of mountains, and snow & ice are true. (?)
answer:
This was a comment on a previous question about whether Pen & Co. would ever visit the Cantons again, to which the answer was "probably not."

The Goodreads layout here makes it unfortunately easy to get mixed up between a comment box and a new question box, in which, like this example, the comment gets separated from its antecedent and bobs out to sea. Watch out for this pitfall when commenting, folks!

Ta, L.

comments.author: Kate Davenport
comments.text: Thanks. I realized that just after I posted it. 🙂

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text: Penric probably wouldn't need to go as far away as his birthplace to show his family or others that snow is real. I know people in SoCal who go "visit" the snow at the top of mountains, for example.

question: Do you think Penric ever takes his family to see Jurald Court or meet his relatives there? Which makes me amazingly grateful that, in all of human history, I live in a time where I can talk to my sister on the other coast any time I like or visit her in 8-10 hours of flying.
answer:
Good question; I'm not sure when or if Pen would ever return to Jurald Court. Travel is difficult in that era, and travel with small kids difficult in any era, so I wouldn't expect travel in the family years. The one reason could have been his mom, but she passed away before Pen ever left Martensbridge. He's not that close to his surviving siblings, or his never-met presumed-by-now nephews or nieces; there might have been cousins or friends, but as time wears on there would be ever fewer of those left alive. He probably still exchanges yearly letters with eldest brother Baron Rolsch, in a stilted Christmas card sort of fashion.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Howard Brazee
comments.text: Different personalities are different. I haven't have contact with my cousins since Kennedy was president. I liked them, but we live different lives.

comments.author: Tricia
comments.text: I've always wanted Pen to take his family back there. They thought so little of Pen....it would be interesting for them to meet him now. Perhaps some adventure/promotion, etc. might take him back to that country?

comments.author: Marti Dolata
comments.text: Pen, I think, has matured well past the adolescent desire to "Show them all!" As Lois said, the ones he would most desire the reaction from have all passed away. The older you get, the less energy you are willing to put into revenging your past hurts, and more content to just go on anyway . The past is the past, be here now, because you are well past the halfway point and have no time to waste on long forgotten pain.

question: All this says to me is need more books! A Piotr history and a current Miles book... actually a half doxen more books, please. (?)
answer:
I'm glad you have enjoyed my work!

The form of this question, which is a very popular one, gives me some odd reflections on the nature of novels existing in a sort of timeless now, leading to some readers picturing writers as also existing in a timeless now, when in fact the 30-something writer Bujold is a very different person than the 70-something (semi-retired) writer Bujold, and not only for the 40 more years of physical wear and tear. It also leads to different perceptions of the books between the older readership who has been following along for decades, and a (hoped-for!) new audience who is discovering them for the first time all at once, as if they'd all been written and published last week. Which ebooks do sort of invite.

Reader-response is endlessly fascinating in its complexity...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Bob Irwin
comments.text: Just wishful thinking. I am re-reading the series, again (more than a dozen times). FTR I give "Mountains of Mourning" ebook to anyone who will take it, as it is a book that was found on Baen's Free List. No other ebooks have been given away, even though Fictiowise gave me a disc will all books sans copy-protection. However, my wife, son, and I have gifted softcover books for this series more than a dozen times. (And... I still have your signature on my PocketPC eReader.)

comments.author: Norine Luker
comments.text: Science fiction and fantasy novels seem to me to be the ones most likely to exist in their own time and space, in a sort of inverse proportion to how much of the current world the author weaves into the story. I found your works about 8 years ago and am happy to read them over and over, even those that had been written gosh knows how many years before. Other favorites of mine have not fared quite so well. One author who set her scifi series in a near future has had to scramble a bit to incorporate technology shifts which make reading the earlier novels jarring. Older fantasy and scifi that I used to really enjoy have suffered from shifts of attitude in the culture that make me sort of cringe. Historical novels are a little more palatable that way, you can't fault Georgette Heyer for incorporating first cousin romances when that was acceptable at the time, but certainly the strength of her writing keeps you going past that first oh yuck. Anyway, I think it is a testament to your writing that so far, rereading your work remains a full and happy transport to the world of the five gods or Barrayar, each time discovering new bits that are fully as engaging as my first experience.

comments.author: Talli Ruksas
comments.text: The almost 70 me gets very different things than the 30s me did from your stories. Besides the pleasure from them are the unexpected insights into myself.

question: I love all the themes and philosophy of religion in your books. I'm wondering, are you personally religious? Is there a particular religion/denomination that you follow or that inspires your work?
answer:
I am not personally religious at this time, having no belief in the supernatural. My family background is Protestant Christian. But I have friends from many other faiths. And, of course, I've read about and studied religions through history, everything from Roman and pre-Roman paganism, medieval Catholicism, other world mythologies, Buddhism, and on and on. It's a fascinating subject, both historically and psychologically. Why do people believe what they believe? (And behave as they behave.) And so on.

Religions, it seems to me, have two major aspects: supernatural assertions, and moral or ethical teachings, advice for living rightly. The latter can have a lot to teach separately from the former, and need not depend for their validity on it.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Mary Berg
comments.text: Yes, being a lifelong atheist myself, I find religion endlessly fascinating. I struggle to understand why people believe. And it forms the basis to my interest in the middle ages.

question: Vorkosigan Idea - Miles does real legislation in Vorkosigan District as I always felt that this was never addressed given as there district always seems to fall behind as Aral and Miles were always off world or at the Capital worrying about Barrayar. Thoughts?
answer:
It's not legislation the VK district needs, it's economic and technological development (the two go hand in hand), education and medical care (ditto), and terraforming. None of which are as easy to implement as waving a pen. People are working hard on all these, but there's so much to do, and it goes "as slowly as a baby grows". If you look from week to week, you see nothing, year to year, some, decade to decade, lots.

Aral, I keep needing to point out, did not have a free hand in the district till Piotr died when Miles was 17, although Cordelia was able to get in some groundwork on the education and medical sides after the reconciliation when Miles was about 6, as "women's work". With a full school generation built and coming on to do the work, when they did get full control they were able to implement quite a lot -- Aral through appointed deputies, as he was always tied up in the capital, Cordelia directly.

By the time we come to the Sergyar Years, Miles, Ekaterin, and Mark as a team are able to do even more, with the human capital painstakingly built up over the prior decade and a half. One cannot run (or build, for that matter) either a hospital or a fusion power plant without highly and expensively trained people. Returning retirees from the military, who had their tech training beyond the district, would be very important starter-leavening for these projects in all eras.

Another point that escapes many readers is that there must always have been quite a bit of what we would dub local democracy going in all over Barrayar, or the place simply would not function. Village moots (of non-uniform sorts, depending on local history) choose the village Speakers, not the district count, ditto town and township councils, city mayors, and so on. A count is the titular head of the district justice system, but even a little math must reveal that he has a whole web of people under him, of which the books only give tiny glimpses, that decide and filter out almost everything but final appeals. The system runs more heavily to build-ups of precedent, bottom-up, than official codes, top-down, though it has both, again varying by district. Granted, the cumulative effect can get almost as weird as British law.

My further thoughts on all this drift from worldbuilding to the more meta level of novel writing (and selling), so this is probably enough for now.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Rowan
comments.text:
"...the cumulative effect can get almost as weird as British law."

As a British person, I approve of this analogy!

comments.author: Elliott Good
comments.text:
I see your point.
I just found it strange how it jumps from miles being in the back country with that dead baby to space shenanigans, still enjoy your work.

comments.author: Marti Dolata
comments.text: Elliot Good - that contrast between mountain and galactic life of which Miles is well aware, gave rise to his dedication to Barryar, and tied him to being Lord Vorkosigan rather than the little Admiral , despite all temptation. That sort of deep character building is what keeps me rereading the series, even after 40 years!!

question: Random questions. I know sorcerers avoid each other but do saints of the Bastard ever get together and talk shop? I’m sure saints of the Mother and the Father must. Which made me wonder, what would the focus of a saint of the Son or the Daughter be?
answer:
I'm sure saints would talk shop, and exchange other mission-critical information, the same as any other professionals. Also to get to complain about their exalted jobs to someone else who understood. Saints are pretty thin on the ground, especially the specialized saints of the Bastard, but local ones would know or know about each other, certainly. (Which leads inevitably to fanficcy musing on what modern communications tools would do to that, heh. Temple websites?)

We did meet one all-but-saint of the Son in the ill-fated shaman Scuolla, for a pointer. Male comradeship, protection, rescue, hunting... there are certainly possibilities.

Saints of the Daughter are an open question. (Well, there was Cazaril, who was certainly one for a time.) Virginity, early childhood education, lyric poetry, temple guards, and spring flowers are rather a grab bag of attributes; there may be more yet to be devised/explored.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Howard Brazee
comments.text: We very early were told about a saint who was a nurse. She would be interesting.

comments.author: Lara Miller
comments.text: If you think about saints of the Daughter in modern times, I can see a lot of scope for their talents! Miracles are needed in lots of elementary schools!

comments.author: Lara Miller
comments.text: Or maybe a modern Saint of the Daughter would protect spring flowers by protecting the environment?

comments.author: Ruth
comments.text: Would She also take an interest in older scholars, assuming they weren't divines of other deities?

question: I just listened to Masquerade again and it got me thinking about demons and dolphins. Do you think a more experienced demon might be able to survive in a dolphin (or whale, etc)? Something with a bigger brain.
answer:
Well, clearly one did, for a while. The question is how much of a prior human mind (i.e., experienced demon) would be shaved off to fit if it were that sort of transfer. Again from that example, quite a lot hung on in bits in poor Ree.

The other is how much controlled chaos shedding it would be possible for the demon to teach the dolphin how to do. Both the dolphins who died in Masquerade died of tumors, etc., engendered by such unshed chaos. Their general ability to survive in the sea may also have been compromised. (Though obviously, wheels coming off their carts or things catching fire around them would not have been issues.) Open question! Or at least, open answer.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text:
It of course remains an open question how much of the demon's... er, spiritual essence remained after it went into the fox (in Penric's Fox.) Clearly enough to remember people it knew before, and enough to care for the fox and her kits.
Possibly enough to work magic on her own -- we saw Des independently take action in Demon Daughter, when Pen wasn't paying attention, so the living entity (human or fox) doesn't need to necessarily have volition about it.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Des does have blanket permission from Pen to act on her own in emergencies, as her reflexes and perceptions are faster, but that's more a measure of the established trust between the pair than the demon ascending in spurts. Granted it would look alarmingly the same from the outside.

With the fox, it would be more the (already humanized and trained) demon ascending in spurts, giving way as needed to the fox to attend her fox business. Dangerous but not malicious.

Ta, L.

question: What message or emotion do you hope readers take away from your books after finishing it?
answer:
I don't write to deliver a "message". That would be rather thudding. My normal process is to set my characters up with an initial problem, turn them loose, and let them show me their stories. Thus, I get to learn as much along the way as they do.

Judging by reviews and fan mail, what my readers take away from their wildly varied readings (of the same texts mind) is, well, first, totally beyond my control. Though I hope it will include, "Huh, that wasn't bad. I wonder what else she's written?" With perhaps a side order of "Eh, maybe I won't slit my wrists today," but that part is also beyond my control, so.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Beth
comments.text:
I picked up Borders of Infinity in a Barnes & Noble about 25 years ago. (I like getting a short story collection as an introduction before committing to the deep dive of a novel. I have commitment issues!) And I've read everything else you've written since then.

I keep wanting more Miles, but I have been very happy with all of your world building. In fact, I crave more Caz almost as much as more Miles.

comments.author: Judy R.
comments.text: More Dag and Fawn, please.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: OK, I still haven’t slit my wrists, and I’ve read most of your works, so the plan seems to be working.

question: Do you suppose that there are any atheists in the world of the five gods?
answer:
Yes, sure.

An unexplored question, is, how would they be treated? The history of our own world offers many models, all a little... off, from the 5GU.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Kate Davenport
comments.text: I’d think it would be hard to be an atheist since these gods are not an abstract concept in their world. Rejecting the gods would be very different than not believing in them. But then people are endlessly capable of believing or disbelieving many thing in the face of overwhelming evidence, so ...

comments.author: Jessica
comments.text: Yeah, I'd think it would be hard to impossible to be an outright atheist. I think there could be people who refused to serve the gods, but people who didn't believe they existed would be a very small, niche group if they existed at all.

comments.author: Carro
comments.text: There are people who turn aside when the gods send them on a mission, there were comments on that for Curse of Chalion about how many were despatched in response to prayers, but for those particular prayers in the story, only Caz arrived.

comments.author: Howard Brazee
comments.text: A question is how gods are defined. We see that there is disagreement with the Bastard. But that could extend to the others as well. In our world we see conversion to Christianity has brought over native gods as angels. There is a long history of people believing in gods who aren't omnipotent.

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text: I often wonder if I would like living in that world or not. I don't see any evidence of gods in our world, but wish there were sometimes.

comments.author: Howard Brazee
comments.text: Some religions have had gods that would be problems. Not counting Cthulhu like beings.

question: I've read DD twice but I get completely different elements when I listen to the audio book! I'm really confused about the Bastard. I thought Quadrenes didn't believe he existed at all but Otta calls him the demon god. Would you help me understand?
answer:
Quadrenes absolutely believe the Bastard exists. It's His classification that is up for hot debate. Is He a god, or is He Something Else? To which the answer is "Yes", but some people can't cope with ambiguity. No coloring outside the lines if you're a Quadrene!

(People being people, they believe lots of other things, too, true and false, but that's outside the scope of this tiny typing box.)

(For even more bizarre real-world religious hairsplitting, look up the origin of the phrase "not one iota of difference" sometime... I wouldn't assert that it's the stupidest argument ever to change history, but it's certainly up there.)

Ta, L.

question: Can we expect a Subterranean Press edition of Demon Daughter?
answer:
Yes. The signed contract just went back.

I don't know exactly when, though. Besides finding it a slot in their list, we still need cover art and, after that, tip-sheet signing (because the signature pages usually feature some of the art.) So, yes, but it might be as much as a year. Because, print publishing. Should be very nice when it gets here, though!

Ta, L.

question: I know people ask you about the books and characters. But I wish in inquire about your health.? You do not have to answer. But i hope you are extra healthy. I worry when my favorite Authors are getting a bit older. I wish you all would live forever.
answer:
I do glance into the mirror occasionally and think, "Eh, not bad for 74." Low bar, perhaps. I have no dramatic health issues at the moment, just a concatenation of minor aggravations of aging. (List available on request, but, really.) Pretty sure I wouldn't like to live forever, but I have no expectation of checking out in the immediate future.

Thank you for your concern!

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I wonder what our expected lifespans would be if granted eternal youth and health. Death would come sooner or later from accident or violence, but individual lifespans could vary greatly.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Some actuarial-minded person once did a calculation, I am told, that the average lifespan in that case would be about 800 years before an accident took one out. Though like medieval death stats, I suspect that might be front-loaded to the younger years...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Lucy
comments.text: I wish to live forever. My to read list is just getting longer so i will need all the time to read all the books.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
Wow, 800 years is longer than I’d have expected. It must depend somewhat on where you’re born; although, if you get through the early years, you’ll probably start moving around.

Glad to hear that you have no expectation of checking out in the immediate future. Neither have I. However, my hope is to die someday very suddenly by surprise: it’s the best way to go.

comments.author: Andrew Brooks
comments.text: Rereading The Fall of Hyperion just now, which contains the caution that one might not want to live forever, particularly if one still ages

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Andrew: Definitely not if you still age! I’m reminded of the song ‘Everdance’ (“Dry bones, sightless, still he dances yet”). However, if you have the ability to live forever, you can choose when to die, instead of getting an unsolicited visit from Death sooner than you would have chosen.

comments.author: Lucy
comments.text: I agree if you age then it will not be fun to live forever.

question: At the end of The Curse Of Chalion, Palli quietly took Cazaril's poem to the Lady Of Spring. As you won't be revisiting Chalion-Ibra, in your mind, what happened to that poem? The way Palli "unobtrusively pocketed" that little slip of paper seemed like foreshadowing of greater things...
answer:
Yep. Palli took it because he was impressed with it, and didn't want Caz to throw it away. Despite his rocky authorial start, I see Caz as a future Quintarian religious poet (in his rare spare time) of lasting merit, in the mode of William Blake but more lyrical. Words that make the hairs stand up on readers' necks, that mean more than they mean, as he tries to fumblingly explain. The Daughter of Spring is a patroness of poetry, after all; he seeks to make flowers to lay on Her altar. And to recapture, as if in a dream, some ghost of that overwhelming moment of union.

He never credits his own poetic power, because it falls so far short of his direct experience of Her.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Sandy
comments.text: Yes, he only sees how short his arrow fell from his target, others see how vastly high it flew. It is often that way with creators.

question: Loois, do you keep track of loose ends you have left behind from each story as you go? (in a list or spreadsheet or some-such)., so that you can glance over the list at some point and say to yourself, "Oh yes, that fits in well with what I'm wondering about now". Or possibly simply have a sense for unfinished business with certain people or situations that may necessitate re-reading before writing further?
answer:
I certainly don't have anything as formal as a spreadsheet (nor would I know how to make one.) I do keep notes, in pencil in a 3-ring binder, where I also do my preliminary outlining as I'm working through story scenes. Mostly my idea-jottings don't go anywhere, even after a couple of pages of further development, but sometimes they do.

If I'm revisiting a character or place in a proposed story, I do reread as needed to refresh my memory.

Ta, L.

question:
I don't see an actual story here, but I wonder how Bosha is doing as a co-parent in the house of two very busy, strong willed adults, a role he must never have envisioned.

Also because someone I know is living with her daughter, son-in-law, and three boys under the age of 8. She is starting to chafe under the apparent assumption that, since she is retired, she will always be available for child care. (?)
answer:
Sounds like a job for fanficwoman...

Though at Adelis-Tanar-Bosha's economic level, there would be plenty of servants around; Bosha also has a job, possibly a new one or two, so I don't think the parallel with your acquaintance would hold.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Kate Davenport
comments.text:
But how much might he want to be involved in raising the children? Especially given that he cannot have children of his own body. I don’t have a good enough read on his personality to know.

Still, I have no doubt they will all create a unique and interesting family group.

comments.author: Norine Luker
comments.text: DId I somehow miss that Tanar had a child???

comments.author: Kate Davenport
comments.text: No. Just assuming.

comments.author: Carro
comments.text: I would think he could be very good at being somewhere else.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
I don't think Bosha would be much for infants, but as the kids got older... I sense that he rather enjoyed teaching swordsmanship to the pages, back when we last saw him, at least enough for his student to be dismayed when his lesson was interrupted. (That much interest couldn't have happened in a vacuum.) I'm not sure he'd still be up for swordsmanship beyond beginner lessons when Adelis & Tanar's kids got old enough, but Bosha has loads of other skills...

I'm assuming A & T have kids, too, but I've not developed the thought. Cousins!

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
“I'm not sure he'd still be up for swordsmanship beyond beginner lessons when Adelis & Tanar's kids got old enough, but Bosha has loads of other skills...”

The kids could learn to how to poison their less-liked relations and neighbours! What fun! I’m reminded of Ronald Searle’s cartoons of St Trinian’s.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: They could start by practising on pigeons, inspired by Tom Lehrer.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
I was actually thinking in terms of writing, ciphers, and forgery, but yeah.

:-), L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Sounds more interesting than school…

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Cousins, what a thought. If that doesn't spring ideas for a new tale, I don't know what will.

question:
Dear Lois,
Some of your books are not easy to find and others appear out of publication. Also, living in Portugal, even if I buy them on British Amazon or American Amazon, I might have to pay high shipping fees depending if they go through customs.
Is there any plan for re-editions of all your books to mass-market paperbacks or hardcovers?
answer:
My ebooks have zero shipping costs, and one platform or another ought to be available in Portugal. Apple Books, Amazon Kindle, and Kobo plus some Google Play Bookstore books should all be pretty international, and have most/all of my backlist available in e-editions. Kindle, I know, offers free downloads of the Kindle reading app, which you should be able to pull in on whatever device you used for this Q. I suspect the other vendors are equally buyer-friendly.

There are no plans for any re-issues of paper editions at this time. Any such would require either a new frontlist novel and the promise of more, or a big media adaptation, neither of which are in the offing just now. The latter may change, the former probably will not. (Reissues as hardcovers are wildly rare -- the writer would have to be Tolkien-level. And mass market paperback sales generally have taken a beating from ebooks, and are struggling at the moment.)

Meanwhile, most of my paper books are still available used, or new through various bookstores (Uncle Hugo's in Minneapolis for a case in point) or online vendors, but then you are back to the shipping-costs issue.

Good luck, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Ana: I live in Spain, and normally buy from Amazon in Spain (amazon.es), which supplies the full range of books in English: both paper books and e-books. I see that amazon.es also reckons to support Portugal, and the language of the site can be switched to Portuguese. I suppose your shipping costs will be much less if you order from amazon.es.

comments.author: Ana Martins
comments.text:
Thank you for your reply. Indeed, I can access the online versions, however, I still prefer paper. I usually use Amazon Spain (with premium the shipping is free for Portugal).
I will continue to try to buy the paperbacks available when possible, I want to have the entire Vorkosigan Saga.
By the way, I would love to see a TV series adaptation for Miles's story. It would be amazing :)

comments.author: Lee at ReadWriteWish
comments.text: L, did you just tease us with talk of an adaptation? Ha!

question: Just wondering if anyone has ever retrieved the jewel stash that her mother told Nykis about in Prisoner of Limnos? Just wondering.
answer:
That is a loose end not yet addressed. It may be something as boring as Adelis-Tanar-Bosha sent to rent/buy the old house and retrieve it, after they were established in power in Thasalon. (Nothing so challenging as what the Arquas had to deal with!) Or it might turn up as a side-moment in some future tale.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text:
Thanks for responding, it would be a fun side-moment in some future tale.

And thank you for reminding me of Captain Vorpatril's Alliance. That is such an entertaining novel. I love seeing Ivan come into his own. The time he spends in the vault/underground bunker with the Arquas, managing to gain some respect from them and consolidating his marriage is a real joy. I love to read/listen to that bit over and over.

comments.author: Talli Ruksas
comments.text: I kind of wondered if it was something Ikos could attempt but he's no longer the only family member who can travel in Thasalon

comments.author: Viola
comments.text: I vote for the second option, a future tale!

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: I find it interesting that the box of jewels has come up in the Demon Daughter Spoiler thread also, and apparently not prompted by this question. Parallel thought processes.

comments.author: Kristen
comments.text: I've been wondering about this too!

question: Any chance of some more Vorkosigan novels or anything on Barrayar?
answer:
Nothing is happening in that direction at this time.

I've mostly been engaged with exploring Penric's possibilities, although with 12 stories it's also getting harder to come up with something new for him that's different from all the others. Otta and Atto were a delightful surprise to us both.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: A delightful surprise to us too!

comments.author: Mitali
comments.text:
A couple of suggestions:
*Maybe Pen could run into a descendant of one of Des's former riders? Or some other aspect of their lives that their respective "layer" in Des could help with, the way Mira helped out in "Dance", or Ruchia in "Prisoner". Preferably (for Pen's sake) nothing medical though.
*Maybe Pen decides to revisit his childhood home/family in the Cantons and gets swept up in a new adventure with old friends and/or in old haunts? Maybe something to do with the new Princess Archdivine of Martensbridge and her court sorcerer?

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Oh, I keep a notebook around the house, to jot ideas as they occur, but most of them die out of my interest after a day, or sometimes an hour. The limiting factor is not ideas, but my energy-of-interest. Which no one else can help me with, I'm afraid.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Conniption Virtue
comments.text:
I *really* wish the Ask An Author had a darn SEARCH function.
If it did, I wouldn't have to wonder if I'm asking an obvious question that had been addressed many times.

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text:
Same here, with regard to the darn search function.

> my energy-of-interest. Which no one else can help me with, I'm afraid.

Oh, I can help! I've developped a new scientific method for that very purpose. I call it "nagging." It's... Hey, where are you going??

question: I'm doing a relisten to the V series and the Audible cover for Barrayar is hands grasping a sword stick. Is this new? It doesn't look familiar.
answer:
This was originally the cover to the second Baen paperback edition of the book, way back when. Cover art by Gary Ruddell.

Ta, L.

question: Does "Knife Children" put a wrap on the Sharing Knife series? I hope not. I was sad when the Vorkosigan series ended, but as much as I enjoyed it, it is the Sharing Knife series I turn to when the days get short and a cozy chair (with a blanket) is most appealing. Then I need my comfort books. I pull volume 1 off the shelf and read again how Fawn sparks new life in world-weary Dag. Such a great story.
answer:
Thank you for the kind words!

I have no idea if something more for The Sharing Knife will emerge in future. I have nothing for it at the moment. But then at the beginning of last October (2023), I'd have sworn I had nothing for Pen & Des, yet at the end of October I suddenly did, so who knows.

Ta, L.

question: Does it annoy you when people tell you their dreams? Last night I dreamed that an insane seafarer showed up, trying to deliver to Penric a boatful of holy animals. Penric was really not sure what to do with them, but the man turned out to be neither exactly insane nor demon-possessed, but instead some kind of botched and inside-out saint. (No idea.) Pen was trying to think what would happen if he brought him to Iroki.
answer:
Ha! I'm more often told daydreams than night dreams.

Though you must have really assimilated the stories, to have them turn up in your unconscious like that. My dreams, alas, are never so useful.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Lara Miller
comments.text:
I do love them a lot! I unwisely stopped in the middle of Demon Daughter and went to bed, and my dreams worked on the problems Pen and Des were facing all night, heh.

Interesting to hear that you don’t get writing ideas from dreams! Long ago in college I took a dream journal/poetry class. We were supposed to write down our dreams and then turn them into poems. I found that the more I focused on remembering my dreams, the longer my journal got until it was threatening to make me late to class every morning! Not that a whole lot of it was writing-applicable, but at least I had a lot to choose from.

comments.author: Marti Dolata
comments.text: Lara Miller - was that "Life as a Poet" taught at UC Santa Cruz? Altho considered an easy high grade by many, it is one of the few classes I still remember vividly after 50 years!

comments.author: Marti Dolata
comments.text: Or was it "Birth of a Poet"? 50 years ago, heh.

comments.author: Lara Miller
comments.text: No, this was at UT Knoxville, about 25 years ago, and the name of the class was something like Dream Poetry, maybe? But it did turn out to be one of my most memorable classes!

question: For people starting to follow your work in the 80s, would they have been able to read Aftermaths in the Far Frontiers publication before Shards of Honor was officially published? I'm about to dive into your work and am entertaining the idea of starting with Aftermaths, but only if the above is true.
answer:
Start with Shards of Honor. The short story "Aftermaths" is appended at the back in its proper chronological place, as a sort of codicil chapter.

It was written after Shards -- Jim Baen pubbed it in advance in what he called his "paperback magazine" Far Frontiers to try to give brand-new writer me a bit of an introduction to readers a month before the novel was due to be first published, for PR.

Are your reading the series for the first time, and are you aiming to follow publication or internal-chronological order?

Internal-chronological order (as well as pub order) is presently listed here:

https://vorkosigan.fandom.com/wiki/Th...

Hope you enjoy!

Ta, L.

comments.author: Marti Dolata
comments.text: After many rereads, it still brings a tear to my eye. That's when I decided you were a favorite author, last century!

question: I've seen that you plot by thinking "what's the worst thing that can happen to THIS guy," but I wanted to ask if you could dive into how this blossoms into a full novel?
answer:
"How do I write my books?" is a short question with an answer much too long for this tiny typing box. I have answered a bunch of questions on my writing process earlier in this column, but, alas, no search function. You might try scrolling back a ways.

But this oft-quoted dictum is actually about justly matching plot to character, not about random character-torture as is often misconstrued. "What's the most interesting thing that could happen to this character next (or, for the more active sorts, "that this character could do next") that I haven't already done?" is closer to my real start-point/s.

I have a bunch of stuff on my process already written out at length in The Vorkosigan Companion (at Kindle https://www.amazon.com/Vorkosigan-Com... and other e-vendors.) This was pulled together in the mid-00s, so the parts where I talk about making printouts on paper of my drafts for editing are now obsolete -- it's pixels all the way since the mid-teens -- but the creative/organizing parts are still much the same.

(Also check your local library -- I see mine still has a copy available.)

I was glancing over the volume for the first time in a while, to be sure it contained what you'd asked for on writing process, and was most struck by what's happened to the ebooks market since the huge game-changing impact of Kindle -- at that time I was still on the early ebooks vendor Fictionwise, later bought up and absorbed by Nook.

Ta, L.

(I should probably make a blog post on the Companion. I bet a lot of newer readers don't know it exists.)

comments.author: Whut
comments.text: Awesome, thanks for the response! I actually JUST learned the Companion existed after stumbling upon and reading Edward James's Lois McMaster Bujold (Modern Masters of Science Fiction).. The Companion was going to be my next read on your work, perhaps after diving back into a few Vorkosigan books first.

question: Is there any hope that the Vorkosigan books will be printed again? I would love nice copies of my favorites but all I can find are used copies in poor condition.
answer:
NESFA Press has the first 8 or so VK books in nice smallish hardcovers, still in print. You can get them (signed) from Uncle Hugo's Science Fiction Bookstore, and some through Dreamhaven Books & Comics. You may also be able to order directly from NESFA. (Which is the New England Science Fiction Association, in full.)

Uncle Hugo's also carries new copies of a lot of my older titles -- you should check their catalog. http://www.unclehugo.com/prod/ah-bujo... You can email the store to inquire about any you don't see there.

There has never been a uniform VK set apart from my indie e-volumes. (Which you can see on that poster to my left in my Goodreads PR photo.)

Amazon, I note, generally has its descriptions of used books for sale stating their condition; "Like new" is probably a useful descriptor though one is of course dependent on the reliability of the sub-vendor providing same.

In general, reprints of backlist books only happen if there is either a big new frontlist book offered expected to sell really well, to carry them in its wake, or a big media adaptation. The currents odds against both are in the "hit by a meteorite" range.

Ta, L.

question: I couldn't find this being asked or discussed before: Were the forms of the four major malices in the books inspired by classic monsters? The Wolf Ridge and Laurel Gap malices may seem obvious but the Glassforge description reminded me of Ben Grimm in FF #1, inspired by clay golems. The Greenspring malice seems comparable to what Victor Frankenstein was attempting to create.
answer:
All the classic monsters are potential models for malices; they are very protean. Malices do have an underlying program or set of compulsions that, though infected and inflected by what they eat, make them not creatures of free will; obligate carnivores, or rather life-vores, with no limiters, sort of like a cross between cancer and unregulated capitalism.

I don't know what FF#1 may be, but I recently watched the excellent anime Frieren: Beyond Journey's End -- the exceedingly creepy demons in that show have the same sort of Turing-machine style I imagined for my malices. They cannot be reasoned with (though they are smart enough to simulate it with clever lying); only destroyed.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Correction, Turing-machine.

Elusive typo, that one. And you can't edit in the comments, hm.

L.

comments.author: Mark Reichert
comments.text: Thank you very much for answering and for posting that correction because I didn't know how to follow up. FF means Marvel's Fantastic Four and I did not in any way mean to suggest that it was an inspiration. That was all my attempt to imagine a construct of earth capable of standing and grabbing and speaking, with human eyes. Thank you again and no need to respond unless I've made a mistake that needs correcting.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text: Ah, thanks for the FF info. I don't follow the comics world much, apart from manga. (And to me, FF means "Falling Free", heh.)

comments.author: Eric Taysom
comments.text: I also enjoyed the demons in Frieren, and appreciate the additional idea/reference for how the malices think.

question:
Thank you for Demon Daughter. I had wondered how you would tell if a new demon (infested?) a young child. I forgot about the fires. LOL. 

My question is: Do you think Penric is known/remembered as a person in Cazaril's time? Or is he just an author on useful books in the chapter libraries?
answer:
Your first question is similar to how one would tell if a new demon infested a cat... :-) You need someone with second/god-sight to diagnose it: saint, petty saint, sorcerer or shaman. And older demon with human personalities and speech would make it more obvious by their ascended behavior, although a very clever demon might even keep that concealed for a while.

I have no idea yet what Pen's relation to Caz's time till be. His books will certainly linger in Temple libraries in all realms where they've had translations, which so far does not include the Ibran peninsula.

A much older Penric interests me, although I don't want to jump prematurely over all the cool story possibilities that lie nearer term.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Kate Davenport
comments.text: He does grow more interesting with every story as does Des, and thanks for that. It is just interesting to wonder if he will become a figure of legend and speculation for his various exploits, just a sketch of himself based on recorded events, or only a name on his books. Regardless, when everyone who ever knew him is gone, who he really is will become something else.

comments.author: Irene Michlin
comments.text: Wouldn’t Penric personality live for a good while inside his demon? So he won’t be just a library name.

comments.author: Judy R.
comments.text: Yes, Penric/Desdemona might -- barring accidents -- survive centuries, it would seem, as part of an increasingly crowded personality complex. Do the older human components become less influential, or does it depend upon their quality instead of age?

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Judy R.: I get the impression from what we’ve already seen that the older personality imprints on a demon seem to withdraw into the background, coming out only when called upon for some specific reason.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
“A much older Penric interests me, although I don't want to jump prematurely over all the cool story possibilities that lie nearer term.”

I’m happy to hear that these cool story possibilities are actively on your mind!

question: Plans for WorldCon this year? I just signed up as a voting member (and virtual attendee) today (2/3/24), just a bit late to nominate. (I also read about the 2023 controversy. Sigh.) (Been reading too much murderbot.)
answer:
When I semi-retired a few years back, the retirement part was mostly from business travel and public speaking, neither of which are comfortable for me anymore. (Although public speaking has managed to follow me home through Zoom, etc., oops. Still not comfortable, but at least no airports.) The writing part, happily, is on the semi side of that hyphen, slowed but not impeded.

So, no Glasgow for me this year, although I have fond memories of the last Worldcon there in the 90s -- Mirror Dance won a Hugo, for a memento still on my shelves.

Ta, L.

question:
Any anticipated date for Demon Daughter in hardcopy? I don't see anything listed on Subterranean Press. Also, similar question on audio version. 

Thrilled that you are continuing to expand this wonderful series.
answer:
I only just signed the contracts for these a few days ago, so it will be a while. I'm guessing 4 - 6 months for the audio and maybe a year for SubPress. It was a surprise entry for both in their publishing queues -- mine as well. Blackstone needs time to record, though if they reuse Ron Miller's cover art that will save a step, and SubPress must contract cover art and also send me 1200 tip sheets/signature pages to sign, which they can't print up till they have the cover art. And so on.

I will make blog post announcements when things go live.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Demon Daughter is currently available for preorder on Audible with a release date of March 12, 2024!!! What I call fast work.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text: Wow, that is fast! Well, Blackstone said during the very brief contract negotiations -- by book 30 or so they're pretty cut and dried -- that they wanted to move it along.

question:
Is there any chance of Penric & Desdemona being published in paperback or hardback compilations?

I hate ebooks
answer:
They already have been. Look for the Baen Books titles Penric's Progress, Penric's Travels, and Penric's Labors.

They've been out for a while, so don't expect to still just find them on store shelves. You'll have to order them, either through your favorite physical bookstore, or online.

(Uncle Hugo's Science Fiction Bookstore here in Minneapolis still carries them, and does mail order.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I’m the opposite, I don’t buy paper books any more. Isn’t it good that we have a choice?

comments.author: Swand
comments.text:
Thank you.

Am in the UK, but managed to order them all here.

Choice is great.

question: Hi Lois! Your books continue to be a great gift and companions along the journey. They have also inspired some great fanfic. One idea that is explored now and then is Gregor awarding Illyan the prefix 'Vor' upon his retirement. It would make thing with Alys easier, and I enjoy exploring the concept but I am not sure it would actually fit. Did you ever consider this, and how did you decide for or against? Merci!
answer:
I decided against, or rather, Simon and Alys did. It's a different political statement if he is offered and politely refuses. And both Alys and Simon not-so-secretly enjoy the transgressiveness of a love affair across caste lines. Cordelia agrees it sets a good example for the younger (and older) generation.

Ta, L.

question:
In “The Hallowed Hunt”, Learned Hallana was annoyed by a rude man, and turned him into a pig temporarily (the spell wore off). It occurs to me suddenly that (a) her ability to do this is rather surprising, and (b) it’s not an ability displayed by any other sorcerers of this world, as far as I remember.

Was it a bit of fun that we shouldn’t take seriously; or can any sorcerer do this kind of thing?
answer:
Early essay, but not incompatible, I think. This sort of hallucination or compulsion spell is normally the province of shamanic magic in this world, as later developed. We do see sorcerer Pen do it much later on (in both book and world time) at a price: the horses he sent on their ways in "Mission", the courier he hypnotized at the end of "Limnos". What price Hallana pays or how she learned it, or if she's special (well, we know that) and it's one of her several god-gifts, not worked out because I didn't need to at that point, plus it wasn't in her viewpoint.

Although I also trust it was a bit of fun.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
Certainly it was a bit of fun, I enjoyed it, and it contributed to the introduction of Learned Hallana.

But the man wasn’t just hypnotized to think he was a pig: as far as I could tell, he became a pig, and everyone around perceived him as a pig (including Hallana herself, again as far as I could tell).

If she put a geas on everyone in the area, she should have bled like a pig, if Penric’s example is anything to go by.

Turning people into animals and back again is an ability traditionally associated with magicians, and I think you allowed her to do that casually, and only later realized that it didn’t really fit into how magic works in your world. Although yes, I suppose it could have been an ability bestowed on Hallana by a god for some particular reason. Which is actually quite a fun idea. (I liked Hallana; I wish we could meet her again.)

I’m not here to give hostile criticism. It just occurred to me that this was a bit of an anomaly in principle, and I couldn’t resist mentioning it.

comments.author: Eric
comments.text: The temptation to say (at great harm to mythology for the sake of a poor pun) that this Circeanly seems worth a thought, is too hard to resist...

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
@ Jonathan -- No, he was just hypnotized. "Else the delusion will wear off in a few hours," as Hallana states.

Granted, the moment might have been under-described, if you were picturing a material pig. But falling to all fours and grunting may indeed be done by any human.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Ah yes, I apologize. Reading the scene in the past, I always assumed that he’d actually been turned into a pig; but, reading it again more carefully, I see that he was indeed suffering from a delusion rather than a transformation. So the only anomaly is that Hallana should have had a nosebleed; which she was spared for some unknown reason.

question: Is there a way to search through questions? I can't find it, yet it's probable my question today has already been asked and answered. What is the necklace you're wearing in your profile pic? I've only just noticed that it's the same you were wearing at the 2012 National Book Festival.
answer:
Yeah, the lack of a search function (and/or my failure to provide tags) is an ongoing problem. In part because some questions do come up repeatedly, and it would be handy to link old answers.

With regards to the necklace, it was the solution my Minneapolis art jeweler friend Elise Matthesen came up with when I brought her my box of assorted award nominee pins I'd accumulated, and wanted them all put together in a way I wouldn't lose. (Again. One Nebula nomination pin was lost when I left a jacket it was pinned to in a restaurant, never recovered, although SFWA kindly provided me with a replacement.) And also that I could conveniently display in such venues where it means something to folks, conventions and booksignings and PR photos and so on. Because pinning them all to a shirt had become impractical. (Mike Resnick, for a while, had a sort of cloth bandolier he could keep all his pins on, which lent him a bandit-like look, and might have given me the idea.) Elise calls it, justly, "wearable art".

I thought there was a little illustrated essay on it somewhere, but a cursory look does not turn up a link. In any case, I am indeed wearing it in the recent PR shot that goes with this blog, where I'm standing in front of the poster with all of Ron Miller's Vorkosigan ebook covers. (Of which people can buy copies, by the way. See https://society6.com/product/the-vork... )

Ta, L.

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text:
It is beautiful. Wearable art indeed. And I found the essay, thanks to your naming the artist. We should both have known where to find the answer! I think this website will be familiar to you:

www[dot]dendarii[dot]co[dot]uk/Photos/lois[dot]html

Which brings another, obvious question to mind: Does the inclusion of the Howard Lovecraft pin make your necklace cursed?

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
P-A asks, "Does the inclusion of the Howard Lovecraft pin make your necklace cursed?"

:-)... Not as I've observed so far. I will say, the Lovecraft bust sculpture of which the pin is a mini-me, designed iirc by Gahan Wilson, comes in ahead as the ugliest genre award trophy ever. I'm rather glad I didn't win one.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text:
I rather like the old trophy actually. It's cute. Unlike the eldritch horror on the left:



Here's what the trophy looks like since 2016:

comments.author: Diana
comments.text:
The illustrated answer to this question was in a blog post linked in a question LMB answered here: https://www.goodreads.com/questions/1...

For future reference, my usual method for searching in corners of the internet that lack a search function is to type search terms into google along with site:[URL of folder to search within]. For example, this time I tried "necklace bujold site:https://www.goodreads.com/questions/" without the quotation marks. The results displayed a bit of text showing the question had been asked and answered before — but led me to the wrong page when I clicked through. I found the link above a harder (but not the hardest!) way.

comments.author: Diana
comments.text: Hmm, it turns out the blog post linked within the page I just linked is about the Hugo award itself! My mistake; Sicart's [dot]ified link is the correct one.

comments.author: Beth
comments.text: I love the necklace. You look great!

question:
LOL, thanks. Not really rhetorical. Real science - not too many aliens, time travel, far off worlds and stuff in real science.

I've read tons of yours, and Fantasy too. Currently working my way through some mysteries that were recommended to me.
Old and tired might be nearer the mark, I was thinking of those writers more. That's the problem with getting old, so does everyone else.?
answer:
This seems to be a comment that was not put in the comment section of whatever Q&A it was responding to, so I have no idea what it's about.

Do be careful, folks, not to get the boxes for comments on old questions and those for new questions mixed up. Responses will get separated from their antecedents and become incomprehensible.

Sorry, L.

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text: To be fair, the comment box is so small and the "Ask Lois McMaster Bujold another question" is placed in such a way as to facilitate such mistakes. I several times started writing a comment in the wrong box myself.

question: Just a comment about the difference of reading a physical book vs an ebook. I purchased "The Curse of Chalion" back in 2001 and held onto it for over 12 years before I got my first Kindle. The book shows all the wear and literally over a hundred times that I've gone through it - but the e-readers can't show any of that. Isn't there something very different from handling a physical book and flipping through pages?
answer:
The biggest difference for me is exactly that -- big -- the ability in ebooks to enlarge the type so my aging eyes can comfortably read it. I've given away scads of old paperbacks and some hardcovers that have print now too small for me.

I do agree about the tactile memory pleasure of a beloved much-read paper book.

My late brother said he'd read Chalion over 40 times. Whatever literary nourishment it was giving him must have been very great, or very needed. I never did tease out from him exactly what.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Henry
comments.text:
I did a review of The Curse of Chalion, and uploaded pictures of my well worn copy.
https://www.goodreads.com/review/show...

comments.author: Henry
comments.text: I recall when I first got the book, that Lois had written a short blurb somewhere about trying to get it published. (Lois - please correct me if I got any of the details wrong). At the time, her Vorkosigan books were all published through Baen Books. She went around to other Publishers for the Chalion book, but nobody wanted to take the risk! When she went back to ask Baen about publishing it, Jim Baen cut her a check for $50K book unread. Guess that's how much he trusted her work. Great investment.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Wrong, sadly. That is a somewhat garbled account of what happened a decade earlier with my first fantasy effort, The Spirit Ring. The Curse of Chalion went to HarperCollins at a successful action (as, where one's agent shops a book to several publishers at once (don't try this at home, kids) for a much tidier sum. It was a 2-book 6-figure contract, iirc, second volume as yet undevised. (It became Paladin of Souls. They'd wanted to make it 3, doubtless hoping for a fantasy trilogy, but I didn't want to commit that far out. In the event they got a trilogy -- well, more of a trio -- later anyway, when I came up with the idea for The Hallowed Hunt.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text:
Same here. During the day, I wear contact lenses, but I can't see up close with them, so I enlarge the font size; in bed, without contact lenses, I have the e-reader two inches from my face, with smaller font size.

The even lighting of modern e-ink readers also works better for me than the glow of an external lamp on paper. As you grow older, you not only get presbyopia but also lose some of your ability to see clearly in low light.

Spoiler alert: Getting old sucks.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
I’m sorry that you need large-print books, even with reading glasses. The Penric stories are an even more impressive achievement, given that you manage to write them despite this problem. At least there’s nothing wrong with your mind; other authors tend to lose their grip mentally as they get older.

I confess that I’ve read The Curse of Chalion only five times. Whereas I’ve read The Assassins of Thasalon ten times already; I wouldn’t nominate it as your best novel, but it’s a good one, and it sits in the Penric series, which I reread as a whole at the drop of a hat.

I’m not sure that I’ve read any book 40 times; but I don’t have a good record of what I read from the 1960s to the 1980s.

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Writing is not actually a problem, given that my laptop also enlarges the print as much as I wish.

My main problem is not near- or far-sightedness, though I am very nearsighted, but macular pucker, aka epiretinal membrane, in my left eye (but not, fortunately, the right) which distorts the, as it were, horizontal and vertical hold (oh, there's an old-tech term that will soon be incomprehensible.) It can't be corrected by lenses, as the problem lies back on the retina. The life-hack that really rescued my reading was realizing I could paint the left lens of my reading glasses opaque with black nail polish, taking that eye off-line but leaving it relaxed and still with light coming in around the edges of the field of view. (Just closing the left eye was a strain, only good for a few minutes, and an eyepatch was uncomfortable.) It works quite well... until something happens to my right eye, I suppose.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
Thanks for the explanation; sorry again about the problem. The eyepatch would be piratical (you could get a toy parrot to go with it), but congratulations on finding a more comfortable solution.

A laptop’s built-in screen is of limited size, but you can attach one or more large separate monitors. I’m not keen on laptops myself, as I don’t need portability; my main computer is a tower system.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Yes, retina problems with eyesight can be very difficult to deal with. I love the ability to adjust font size on eBooks. Another, fortunately future potential issue is that my library of physical books is so large that if I ever have to downsize substantially, possibly even to one room in some sort of "home", I could never bring them all. But with an eReader and eBooks (and library resources to add to it) I have far less fear of a day when I can't read the books I love the most because of lack of access.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: I have paper books that I’ve owned since the 1960s and 1970s. In some cases the pages are in danger of falling out by now. E-books are more durable, as long as software still exists that is capable of reading them.

question: Hi Lois, this is Albert from Minneapolis, we have met at CONvergence many years ago. I would like to know if you have been approached either via agent or directly on adapting your Barrayar stories to screen. Miles and his family are very interesting characters and that universe has many intriguing facets so I was wondering if you have any thoughts on it on TV or big screen?
answer:
No offers since the mid-90s, which are, good grief, almost 30 years ago now.

My ideal would of course be a miniseries that hews closely to the books.

That said, I think Falling Free could be a good stand-alone animated feature film -- it could all be squeezed into 90 minutes without losing much. The Spirit Ring likewise, same reason, but those are not the books most folks are thinking about.

But the sad truth is, once a production company licensed any work of mine, it would be run through multiple committees of endless compromises on its route to release, and what came out the other end probably wouldn't have much resemblance to my books. There are also the Problems of Miles, not only his physical appearance but the fact (shared with my other point of view characters) that much is what of most interest to readers including the humor is actually happening inside his head, in his thought-stream, and making that visual would be very hard.

All that said, it could be a great (if expensive) advertisement for my books...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Whenever I see an on-screen version of a book, the book is almost always better. Occasionally the translation to screen works well enough; for example, with “Good Omens” and the Harry Potter series. In both cases, I think the author was given more influence than usual on the screen version.

comments.author: Seantheaussie
comments.text:
"much is what of most interest to readers including the humor is actually happening inside his head"

Why I don't actually want most of my favourite books filmed.

comments.author: Laureen Hart
comments.text:
YES! Books that take place in the mental space are hard to translate to screen.
And, as Lois says, the result would probably bear no relation to the books. Like the James Bond Movie "Spy who loved me" Only the title was by Ian Fleming - the entire movie was not related to his novel.

Princess Bride comes close to the book.
Accidental Tourist as well.
But most are terrible.

comments.author: Martha
comments.text: For me, the LotR movies made the books readable. Trying to read them pre-movies was an exercise in "OK, who the @#$% is Mithrandir, and why should I care about him??"-type confusion. The movies put faces to the names and gave it all a cohesive context that the text sorely lacks. Another example: the twist at the end of the last Hunger Games book works much better on-screen than it did on-page. But those sorts of exceptions are pretty rare, and like Lois, I have a hard time imagining how the inside of Miles' head could be translated to a visual medium.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text: Martha: I first read LotR in 1968, when I was 14, found it readable enough then, and reread it I don’t know how many times before the films came out. Peter Jackson’s films make quite a nice visual supplement to the book in places, but they get the story wrong in many small and not-so-small ways. Various times I’ve started to watch the films but became irritated and reread the book instead. Although I have watched the films all the way through at least once.

comments.author: Jerri
comments.text: Like Jonathan, I first read LotR (and The Hobbit) long before the movies were thought of, probably about 1971 or so, and re-read many times. And enjoyed the visual supplement to the books, but was frustrated by the changes made, many of them foolishly/needlessly. But different strokes for different folks.

question: Hi Lois, I love your books but for some reason haven't gotten around to reading your Penric series yet. Since I can only listen to audiobooks at the moment due to health reasons, I would be interested to know if you plan to release the four omnibus books (Penric's Progress/Travels/Labors/Intrigues) as audiobooks?
answer:
All the Pen & Des stories are available from Blackstone Audiobooks and its vendors under their individual titles. (Well, the newest one, "Demon Daughter" will be coming later this year.)

As an example, Apple Books has this lineup:

https://books.apple.com/us/author/loi...

Also to be found on Amazon, Kobo, Nook, Google Play, and possibly other vendors. And don't forget Downpour.com -- Blackstone's own audio download distribution outlet.

For anyone wondering where to start, the internal chronology of the series goes:

“Penric’s Demon”
“Penric and the Shaman”
“Penric’s Fox”
“Masquerade in Lodi”
“Penric’s Mission”
“Mira’s Last Dance”
“The Prisoner of Limnos”
“The Orphans of Raspay”
“The Physicians of Vilnoc”
The Assassins of Thasalon
“Knot of Shadows”
“Demon Daughter”

Hope this helps --

Ta, L.

comments.author: Martini
comments.text: Thank you!

question: Do you favor any particular tech for writing stories or keeping notes? There are so many different types of paper-like writing tablets, writing apps, and so forth available these days. I wondered what you as an author use for getting ideas written down, organized, and eventually sent out. Any recommendations of what to use or perhaps avoid? Has tech changed your process over the years?
answer:
Since I wrote the first drafts of my first 10 published novels in pencil in a 3-ring binder, I am unimpressed by all the futzing over writing paraphernalia. The penciled drafts were subsequently transcribed for submission drafts onto a succession of other tools over time -- typewriter, Coleco Adam, KayPro II, whatever came after that in the way of desktops, couple of Dell laptops, and now, my nice compact Lenovo Thinkpad. I gradually graduated to writing first drafts from the penciled notes/outlines directly on my computer when I first got my own writing space, a little computer stand in my bedroom. Around 1993.

Environment's improved, step by step and move by move over the years, but I still do my prelim notes in pencil in a binder. College-ruled punched paper bought, usually, in the grocery store.

So no, tech has not changed my process much, although I make happy and heavy use of the built-in spelling check and thesaurus Word comes with. My first drafts go down faster -- though in the same chunks, scene or part-scene spurts -- but I do (and need) a lot more micro-editing, so no time is saved overall. I almost never make printouts anymore. I'm very happy not to have to mail manuscripts. After the notes (still in pencil in a 3-ring binder) it's pixels all the way to publication now.

Ta, L.

comments.author: Christine
comments.text: Have you ever tried dictation software?

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
No. Although I am averse, being not especially an aural person, and needing the visual feedback loop of writing the words down in whatever mode. 40 years have made my habits rather ingrained. Also, I don't have enough fingerprints left to do fingerprint pattern recognition security software...

Ta, L.

comments.author: Karenhunt
comments.text: I love the fingerprint bit - I neither take ink well on my fingers for the old style nor can do the fingerprint scanners. Next-to-last time they got a "good enough" scan off of exactly one finger. Last time, the print they got off that finger didn't match the scan they had...

question: I'm reading the latest Penric & Desdemona novella & loving it? More importantly, I've posted a recommendation on my long-established author's website. Then I reposted it on my Facebook & Twitter/X websites. https://LaerCarroll.com.
answer:
Thank you so much! Word of mouth (now turbo-boosted by the internet) has always been the lifeblood of my career.

Ta, L.

question: Hello. I'm a huge fan from France, both for your SciFi and fantasy works, and I wondered if there is a particular reason for your "Penric & Desdemona" series to not being translated yet in french ? I asked your publisher here several times and never been answered. Even if I could, of course, read these stories in english, it makes me sad and a bit angered that your french readers can't access them. Many thanks.
answer:
I'd be delighted to sell the Penric series to a French publisher, now collected into 4 tidy book-sized volumes for just this purpose:

Penric's Progress
Penric's Travels
Penric's Labors
Penric's Intrigues

Operators are standing by... (At my agent's French agent, Agence Litteraire Lenclud, which French publishers presumably know how to find.)

But this decision is up to the publishers, who have to choose books that they think will sell well, so they don't sink their business. I have absolutely no guess how my works fall in that decision matrix, except that if Sauron had the Ring we would know it I lack evidence that I'm a top seller in France. Persistent, consistent, maybe, but not breakout. C'est la vie.

Yes, that's a negative feedback loop. Welcome to publishing. (But not e-publishing, which is why I'm now a hybrid indie-traditional writer.)

Ta, L.

comments.author: Jonathan Palfrey
comments.text:
The original three novels of the World of the Five Gods seem to be available in French and Spanish, but not the Penric series, which is a shame.

Of course, you could ask Penric to translate the stories himself, in his copious spare time…

comments.author: Lois Bujold
comments.text:
Well, Pen speaks no English, but I suppose Darthacan and Ibran count for something...

:-), L

comments.author: Pierre-Alexandre Sicart
comments.text: "Penric's Demon" was translated by Mélanie Fazi (who's very good). "Le Démon de Penric" can be found in Chalion - L'Intégrale, published in 2016 by Bragelonne.

question: In your review of Heaven's Official Blessing Vol 1, you had wondered which word was translated as "Jeeze!". The word was likely もう mou, which literally means an acknowledgment of a change of state, with a connotation of surprise. The translator of Ascendance of a Bookworm, Quof, defended "Geez" on J-Novel Club Forums. My questions are: Have you interacted with any translators of your work? If so, what was it like?
answer:
With respect specifically to "Jeeze!", one has to know it was a politer contraction of "Jesus", like darn to damn, from a time when such expressions were considered blasphemous. (We have moved on to different forbidden/violated vocabularies now.) Very anachronistic in fantasy ancient China. One might have gotten away with "Buddha!", I suppose... It's possible the young translator had never heard the word used as anything but a noise of surprise, and did not know its history. But yes, you confirm my suspicion that the original word in Chinese had none of the above historical baggage.

I have interacted with a few of my translators, mostly since email; it was not feasible before. Though my Japanese translator did get to visit me briefly back in my early days in Marion, OH, when she was in the States to visit her brother at Cornell. I remember specifically trying to explain the idiom "he pursed his lips" by fetching out a little string bag I happened to have, and demonstrating its closure. But again, one has to know that's what archaic purses looked like.

It's generally an exercise in mutual frustration, especially when only the one side speaks both languages.
